(RASHMI DHYANI PANT)        (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS        COURT MASTER (NSH)
(signed order is placed on the file)
Crl. Appeal @ SLP(Crl.) No.7022/2026 6

--- Page 186 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 19059 of 2025)
SUPTAN MARAK  APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF TRIPURA  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Under the impugned judgment, the High Court has
affirmed the appellant’s conviction and sentence under
Section 342 and 323 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and
Section  4  of  the  Protection  of  Children  from  Sexual
Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012 imposed by the Trial Court vide
its judgment and sentence dated 13.12.2022.
3. Having heard the learned counsel for the appellant
on merits, we are not inclined to interfere with the
impugned judgment of the High Court, as victim is aged 12
years and is a partially and mentally retarded girl, and
she has supported the prosecution and her version has been
duly corroborated by her mother (PW-2) and her younger
sister (PW-4). Dr. Helina Debbarma (PW-14) has examined the
victim  and  found  that  the  condition  of  victim  was
precarious  and  she  complained  of  pain  in  vulva  with
excessive bleeding, repeated vomiting etc.; and the victim
1

--- Page 187 ---
was not in a condition to move even; and excessive blood
was oozing out through vaginal intorious and her hymen was
freshly torn.
4. In view of the above evidence, even if the DNA
profiling of the blood stains found on the victim’s cloths,
is not found conclusively belonging to the blood of the
appellant, we are of the considered view that the unshaken
oral testimony of the POCSO victim duly corroborated by her
mother and her younger sister, cannot be disbelieved only
on this ground.
5. Accordingly,  the  appellant’s  conviction  under
Section 4 of the POCSO Act is maintained.
6. It is argued that the Trial Court and the High Court
as well, have not specifically referred to the applicant’s
conviction under Section 4(2) of the POCSO Act, therefore,
the maximum sentence of life imprisonment imposed on the
appellant is not sustainable inasmuch as the appellant will
have to undergo imprisonment for the remainder of his life
and he shall never be released from jail.
7. It is submitted that non-mentioning of sub-Section
(2) of Section 4 of the POCSO Act, in particular, vitiates
the sentencing order passed by the Trial Court as well as
the High Court. It is also submitted that the appellant was
aged about 33 years at the time of occurrence having no
antecedents.  Hence,  there  is  a  possibility  of  the
appellant’s  reformation  and  thus,  imposition  of
imprisonment for the remainder of the life is not warranted
2

--- Page 188 ---
in the facts of the case.
8. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State
has  opposed  the  submissions.  According  to  him,  the
appellant has been found guilty of commission of offence
under the POCSO Act concurrently by the Trial Court and the
High Court, therefore, no leniency should be shown.
9. Although, we are not convinced with the submission
made by learned counsel for the petitioner that in the
absence of specific reference to sub-Section 2 of Section 4
of the POCSO Act by the Trial Court and the High Court, the
sentencing part is vitiated, because any reference to
Section 4 would attract the provisions contained in sub-
Section (1) and/or sub-Section (2) of Section 4, depending
upon the facts of the case which attracts any of these sub-
Sections.
10. Once it is established that the victim was less than
sixteen years of age, the only provision with which the
appellant could have been convicted and sentenced, is sub-
Section (2) of Section 4 of the POCSO Act. Therefore, non-
mentioning of particular sub-Sections of Section 4 of the
POCSO Act, would not render the sentencing part of the
Trial Court order and affirmed by the High Court, as
illegal or vitiated.
11. At the same time, we feel that considering the age
of the appellant and lack of antecedents, as also for the
fact that there is no material that his conduct in jail so
far is not good, the sentence for remainder of the life
3

--- Page 189 ---
which is the maximum sentence which can be imposed under
sub-Section (2) of Section 4, deserves to be reduced to the
minimum sentence of twenty years which can also be imposed
under sub-Section (2) of Section 4 of the POCSO Act.
12. Accordingly, we allow the Criminal Appeal in part
and  reduce  the  sentence  of  life  imprisonment  i.e.
imprisonment for the remainder of the life to a sentence of
rigorous imprisonment for twenty years.
13. Accordingly, while maintaining the conviction of the
appellant for the offences under Section 4(2) of the POCSO
Act, we modify/reduce the sentence of life imprisonment
i.e. for remainder of the life to rigorous imprisonment for
twenty years.
14. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  
 ………………………………………………………J.
             [PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA]
   
    ………………………………………………………J.
           [SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR]
NEW DELHI;
July 21, 2026
4

--- Page 190 ---

--- Page 192 ---
CRL.A. NO@SLP(CRL.).11491 OF 2026  1 
 
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA 
CRIMINAL APPEALLATE JURISDICTION 
 
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(S).           OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.)No.11491 of 2026) 
 
TAUSIF @ BHURIO          …APPELLANT 
 
VERSUS 
 
STATE OF GUJARAT       …RESPONDENT 
 
  
O R D E R 
  
 
1. Leave granted. 
2. The present appeal arises out of the order dated 
4th May 2026 passed by the High Court of 
Gujarat at Ahmedabad in R/Criminal Misc. 
Application (For Cancellation of Bail) No.17294 
of 2021, whereby the High Court allowed the 
respondent-State's application for cancellation 
of the appellant's bail. 
3. The brief facts giving rise to the present appeal 
are as follows: 
3.1. On 20 th January 2021, an offence was 
registered against the appellant -accused and 
other accused persons by DCB Police Station, 

--- Page 193 ---
CRL.A. NO@SLP(CRL.).11491 OF 2026  2 
 
Vadodara, vide FIR No.11196010210001 of 
2021, for offences punishable under Sections 
3(1)(i) & (ii), 3(2), 3(4) and 3(5) of the Gujarat 
Control of Terrorism and Organised Crime Act, 
2015.1 
3.2. The appellant is FIR accused no.20 and is 
alleged to be part of a crime syndicate named 
the "Bicchu Gang". The allegations in the FIR 
are that the gang forcibly acquires land and 
extorts money, and that in order to  create an 
atmosphere of fear in the city of Vadodara, its 
members commit murder, kidnapping, robbery, 
dacoity and fraud. 
3.3. The appellant was arrested on 20th January 
2021 and thereafter remanded to judicial 
custody. 
3.4. The Trial Court, vide order dated 28th June 
2021, enlarged the appellant on regular bail. 
The Court observed that, admittedly, no other 
offence had been registered against the 
appellant after 1st December 2019 which is the 
date on which the GCTOC Act, 2015 came into 
force, so as to  constitute "continuing unlawful 
 
1 Hereinafter, “GCTOC Act, 2015” .  

--- Page 194 ---
CRL.A. NO@SLP(CRL.).11491 OF 2026  3 
 
activity". It further noted that the investigation 
had concluded, the charge -sheet had been 
submitted, and the other co -accused had 
already been enlarged on bail. 
3.5. After being enlarged on bail, the appellant 
and his now wife eloped. 
3.6. As a result, on 21 st July 2022, the 
appellant's brother -in-law, i.e., his wife's 
brother, filed FIR No.11196017220694 of 2022 
under Sections 323, 294(b), 506(2) and 114 of 
the Indian Penal Code, 1860 at Panigate Police 
Station, alleging that the appellant came to his 
house and inquired about his sister, that this 
led to an altercation in which the appellant 
abused him and beat him with a stick. 
3.7. Subsequently, on 3 rd August 2023, the 
appellant settled the matter with the members 
of his wife's family and was acquitted in the said 
FIR by the Trial Court. The brother -in-law, 
being the complainant in that FIR, admitted in 
cross-examination that the parties had reached 
a se ttlement outside the Court and that no 
dispute persisted. He further clarified that only 
a general quarrel had taken place and that the 
appellant had not beaten him. 

--- Page 195 ---
CRL.A. NO@SLP(CRL.).11491 OF 2026  4 
 
3.8. The State preferred R/Criminal Misc. 
Application (For Cancellation of Bail) No.17294 
of 2021 before the High Court under Section 
439(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, 
seeking cancellation of the bail granted to the 
appellant by the Trial Court on 28th June 2021 
in the FIR registered under the GCTOC Act, 
2015. 
3.9. The High Court, by the impugned order, 
cancelled the appellant's bail in the offence 
under the GCTOC Act, 2015, on the ground that 
the appellant had violated the conditions of bail 
by taking undue advantage of his release and 
indulging in another offe nce, having regard to 
the subsequent FIR filed by his brother -in-law 
as noted above. 
3.10. Aggrieved, the appellant is before us. 
4. We have heard learned counsels for the parties. 
5. The learned counsel for the appellant submits 
that the subsequent FIR relied upon by the High 
Court for cancelling bail was nothing but a FIR 
lodged by the appellant's own brother -in-law, 
arising out of a family dispute over the 
appellant's marriage, and that such a FIR 
cannot be treated as a fresh offence justifying 

--- Page 196 ---
CRL.A. NO@SLP(CRL.).11491 OF 2026  5 
 
cancellation of bail granted in a separate and 
unrelated case. It is further submitted that the 
said matter has since been amicably settled 
between the parties, the complainant himself 
has admitted in cross -examination that no 
dispute survives, and the app ellant stands 
acquitted by the Trial Court.  
6. Learned counsel for the respondent-State, fairly 
and candidly, does not dispute the aforesaid 
position and admits that the subsequent FIR 
has indeed been settled between the parties and 
has resulted in the appellant's acquittal. 
7. Having regard to the above, we are of the view 
that the High Court erred in cancelling the 
appellant's bail solely on the basis of the 
subsequent FIR filed by his brother-in-law. That 
FIR arose out of a private family dispute and not 
out of any fresh criminal activity of the kind 
contemplated for cancellation of bail granted in 
the earlier case under the GCTOC Act, 2015.  
8. As noted above, the said FIR has since been 
settled between the parties, the complainant 
himself has admitted before the Trial Court that 
no dispute survives, and the appellant has been 
acquitted. It is well settled that bail once 

--- Page 197 ---
CRL.A. NO@SLP(CRL.).11491 OF 2026  6 
 
granted should not be cancelled in a mechanical 
manner, and there must be some supervening 
circumstance showing misuse of liberty. 
9. In these circumstances, the present appeal is 
allowed. The impugned order dated 4 th May 
2026 is set aside, and the bail granted to the 
appellant by the Trial Court vide order dated 
28th June 2021 stands restored. The appellant 
is in custody. He shall be enlarged on bail  
forthwith on such terms and conditions as may 
be determined by the Trial Court. 
10. Pending applications, if any, to be 
disposed of.  
 
……………………………….J. 
[VIKRAM NATH] 
 
……………………………….J. 
[SANDEEP MEHTA] 
 
 
NEW DELHI; 
JULY 15, 2026 

--- Page 198 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

(Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.8165 of 2026)
  NIHAL YADAV                 APPELLANT(S)

4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the material available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 07.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  seek  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court finds that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail. Further, it is clarified
that if the appellant, in any manner whatsoever, tries to
3

--- Page 201 ---
influence,  threaten  or  intimidate  the  private
respondent/complainant or any of the witnesses, it shall
be open to the Trial Court to cancel the bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 09.04.2026 is
set aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

            (SANJAY KAROL)
                            ........................J.

JULY 21, 2026.

2. On 10.02.2023, notice(s) were issued to the

appellant(s) would not be entitled to the interest
for a period of delay.

--- Page 209 ---
2
parties submit that when it comes to compensation,
the  appellant(s)  are  entitled  to  receive  the
compensation determined by this Court in the order
dated 21.09.2017. To that extent, there is no
dispute between the learned counsel appearing for
the parties.

issuing notice(s), it has been made clear that the
period of delay will be excluded for computation of
interest payable on the enhanced compensation, now
being determined by this order.

allowed in part.

computing the interest payable to the appellant(s),
the period of delay namely, 2834 days in filing
Regular First Appeal before the High Court and 229
days in filing the Civil Appeal No. 9210 OF 2026
before this Court and 2611 days in Regular First
Appeal before the High Court and 161 days filing
the Civil Appeal No. 9211 OF 2026 before this Court
are excluded and the compensation is paid/deposited

without fail.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
   CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
  (Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.5896 of 2025)
                              
ANITHA                                               APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
THE STATE REPRESENTED BY THE 
INSPECTOR OF POLICE    RESPONDENT
WITH
  CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
  (Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.6542 of 2025)
                              
VARUN                                               APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
THE STATE REPRESENTED BY THE 
INSPECTOR OF POLICE    RESPONDENT
WITH
  CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
  (Arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.6441 of 2025)
                              
MARIAPPAN                                               APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
THE STATE REPRESENTED BY THE 
INSPECTOR OF POLICE & ANR.       RESPONDENTS
R1 : THE STATE REPRESENTED BY THE 
INSPECTOR OF POLICE 
R2 : ASAIMANI
O R D E R
Heard learned counsels for the parties.
2. Leave granted.

--- Page 214 ---
2
3. The appellants before us are father, mother and son. They had
been  convicted  by  the  Trial  Court  vide  its  judgment  dated
15.12.2021 in STC No.55 of 2020 under Sections 294(b) and 323 of
the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for short, the “IPC”) and sentenced
to fine of Rs.500/- (Rupees Five Hundred) and in default of
payment of fine, to undergo simple imprisonment of one week under
Section 294(b) of the IPC and to undergo simple imprisonment of
six months and to pay a fine of Rs.1,000/- (Rupees One Thousand)
and in default, to undergo simple imprisonment of one week under
Section 323 of the IPC. The same was upheld by the Appellate Court
as well as by the High Court.
4. Learned counsel for the appellants submitted that the present
cases arise from a minor incident due to an unfortunate quarrel,
which basically resulted in some injuries on the person of the
victim-complainant.  However,  it  was  submitted  that  now  the
appellant-Anitha in Appeal arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.5896 of
2025 and appellant-Mariappan in Appeal arising out of SLP(Crl.)
No.6441 of 2025, who are husband and wife, have undergone three
months  of  incarceration  whereas,  the  son-Varun,  who  is  the
appellant in Appeal arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.6542 of 2025, has
been protected by the Court and has not faced incarceration on the
ground that he is under nineteen years of age.
5. Learned counsel for the appellants submitted that the Court
may take a sympathetic view in the matters.
6. Learned counsel for the respondent(s)-State submitted that
the conviction of all the appellants is based on cogent evidence
and logical reasoning, which has been upheld by the Appellate

--- Page 215 ---
3
Court as well as by the High Court and the same does not need any
interference. However, it was his stand that considering the age
of the appellant-Varun, the Court may take a call with regard to
his release under the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958.
7. Having  considered  the  matters  holistically  from  various
angles, we find that ends of justice would be served by upholding
the conviction and modifying the sentence against the appellant-
Anitha in Appeal arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.5896 of 2025 and
appellant-Mariappan in Appeal arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.6441 of
2025 to the period already undergone whereas, upholding the
conviction and modifying the sentence against the appellant-Varun
in Appeal arising out of SLP(Crl.) No.6542 of 2025 by directing
his release under the Probation of Offenders Act,1958. It is
ordered accordingly.
8. These appeals stand disposed of in the aforementioned terms.
9.  Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
 ……………………………………………………………………J.
   [AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH]
…………………………………………………………………………J.
              [MANMOHAN]
NEW DELHI
21st JULY, 2026

--- Page 216 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.3350 OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.6935/2026]
JASVIR SINGH                 APPELLANT
VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB & ANR.   RESPONDENTS
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The  High  Court  of  Punjab  &  Haryana  at
Chandigarh,  by  the  impugned  judgment  and  order
dated 17th March, 2026, has rejected the appellant’s
prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest. 
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No.18 of
2025 dated 05 th February, 2025 registered at Police
Station Balachaur, District SBS Nagar under Section
85 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023.
4. We  have  heard  learned  counsel  appearing  for
the appellant as well as respondents.
5. The  application  for  anticipatory  bail  has  been
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6935/2026 1

--- Page 219 ---
rejected only on the ground that while on interim bail,
he misused the concession by beating his wife.
6. Appellant  is  an  army  man.  The  allegation  of
beating is seriously disputed before us.
7. Be that as it may, the offence alleged to have
been committed by the appellant is triable by a
magistrate.
8.  Having regard to such facts and circumstances,
we are of the considered opinion that  the appellant’s
detention for custodial interrogation is not necessary
and that he may be admitted to an order for grant of
bail in anticipation of arrest.
9. We,  accordingly,  set  aside  the  judgment  and
order impugned in this appeal.
10. It is directed that in the event of the appellant
being arrested, he shall be released on bail on terms
and conditions to be imposed by the trial court.
11. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,
directly or indirectly, by making inducement, threat
or promise, dissuade any person acquainted with the
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6935/2026 2

--- Page 220 ---
facts of the case from disclosing such facts to any
police officer or to the court.
12. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in  this
order and grant of bail to the appellant in anticipation
of arrest will not be treated as findings on the merits
of the case.
13. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed  on  the
aforesaid terms.
14. Pending application(s), if any, stand disposed of.
…..……………………...J.
[DIPANKAR DATTA]  
…………………………..J.
[SHEEL NAGU]
New Delhi;
July 22, 2026.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6935/2026 3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
[Arising out of SLP (CRL) NO. 3968-3974/2025]
JAGTAR SINGH JOHAL @ JAGGI                    Appellant(s)
VERSUS
NATIONAL INVESTIGATION AGENCY                 Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
We have heard the learned Senior counsel appearing
for the appellant and the learned Additional Solicitor
General appearing for the respondent.
Arguments have been advanced by the learned counsel
for the parties on merits. Incidentally, a submission has
been made by Mr. Basant, learned Senior counsel appearing
for the appellant that inasmuch as five of the appeals have
not been decided on merits by the High Court, in the event
that the said appeals are directed to be remitted to the
High Court to be decided afresh on merits, the remaining
two matters shall also be remitted to the High Court. He
further submitted that the aforesaid submission is being
made based upon the premise that the allegations against
the appellant are overlapping and similar in all the
appeals. 
Learned Additional Solicitor General appearing for
1

--- Page 229 ---
the respondent submitted that the allegations are distinct
in each appeal and not overlapping. He submitted that
taking note of the fact that this Court has already taken a
view in other similar matter(s) to remit and decide the
appeals on merits whenever they have been rejected on the
ground of delay, the other two matters will have to be
decided on merits by this Court, as they have already been
decided on merits by the High Court.
Considering the submissions made and taking note of
the fact that the present appeals are pending before us
from the year 2025, we deem it appropriate to give one more
opportunity to the appellant to argue all the appeals
before the High Court on merits and in accordance with law.
In such view of the matter, the impugned order is set
aside and the matters are remitted to the High Court to be
decided afresh on merits and in accordance with law without
going into the question of condonation of delay. We request
the High Court to make an endeavour to dispose of the said
appeals within a period of two months from the date of
receipt of a copy of this order.
All issues are left open to be raised and argued by
the contesting parties.
The Criminal Appeals are disposed of, accordingly
with the aforesaid directions.
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
2

...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 15, 2026.

--- Page 238 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
EXTRA-ORDINARY APPELLATE JURISDICTION
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (C)NO. 17375/2025
NALINI & ORS.                                      PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
THE BRANCH MANAGER UNITED INDIA INSURANCE
CO LTD       RESPONDENT(S)
 
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition arises out of the impugned
judgment dated 12.07.2024 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at
Bengaluru in Miscellaneous First Appeal No.5175 of 2018 (MV-D).
2. The facts of the case reveal that a claim petition was
preferred by the dependents of the deceased, Surendra Acharya, son
of Late Appayya Acharya , who died in a road accident that took
place on 31.10.2013 at about 6:45 p.m. on account of the rash and
negligent driving of a Scooter bearing Registration No. KA-19-EC-
1420. The legal heirs instituted a claim petition before the Motor
Accidents Claims Tribunal, D.K., at Mangalore. After considering
the evidence on record, the Tribunal arrived at the conclusion that
the deceased met with an accident and sustained serious head
injuries, multiple fractures and other injuries all over his body.
The deceased was shifted to the nearby Shrinivas Hospital, Mukka ,
where he remained bedridden before succumbing to the injuries on
24.04.2014. The documents relating to his treatment are also on
PETITION(S) FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C) NO(S).  17375/2025

--- Page 239 ---
2
record. On consideration of the same, the Trial Court while
allowing the claim petition, awarded compensation of Rs.18,25,000/-
along with interest at the rate of 6% per annum from the date of
the petition till realization.
3. Respondent No.1, United India Insurance Company Limited, being
aggrieved by the award passed by the Trial Court, preferred an
appeal under Section 173(1) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988,
against the award dated 14.03.2018. The learned counsel appearing
for the Insurance Company submitted that there was no medical
evidence on record to prove the cause of death, and that the body
of the deceased was not subjected to post-mortem examination to
establish a nexus between the injuries sustained on 31.10.2013 and
his death on 24.04.2014. Paragraph No. 3 of the impugned judgment
dated 12.07.2024, reads as under: 
“3. Shri  O.Mahesh,  learned  counsel  for  the
appellant,  argued  that  there  is  no  medical
evidence on record to prove the cause of death and
the body of the deceased was not subjected to a
postmortem  to  establish  a  nexus  between  the
injuries sustained on 31.10.2013 and the death on
24.04.2014 at his residence.  Therefore, in the
absence of any material to substantiate that the
deceased succumbed to accidental injuries, the
impugned award passed by the Tribunal is not
legally sustainable.” 
4. Based upon the submissions made by the learned counsel for
Respondent No.1–Insurance Company, the High Court, in paragraph No.
6 of the impugned judgment dated 12.07.2024, observed as under:   
“6. The charge sheet materials at Ex.P.1 to 4
indicate that the deceased sustained accidental
injuries on 31.10.2013 to his head, which is also
PETITION(S) FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C) NO(S).  17375/2025

--- Page 240 ---
3
evident from Ex.P.7. The accident took place on
31.10.2013 at about 6:45 p.m., and he died at his
residence on 24.04.2014. The claimants have not
produced any medical evidence to establish a nexus
between the injuries sustained on 31.10.2013 and
the death on 24.04.2014 at his residence. In the
absence of any material to substantiate the same,
the impugned award passed by the Tribunal is not
legally  sustainable.  Accordingly,  I  pass  the
following:
ORDER
i. The appeal is allowed.
ii. The impugned judgment and award is
set-aside and consequently the claim
petition is dismissed.
iii.  The  appellant  is  permitted  to
withdraw the amount in deposit before
this Court.”
5. This Court has carefully gone through the aforesaid paragraph
No. 6. The High Court has not considered the evidence on record
while allowing the appeal preferred by the Insurance Company. The
High Court has brushed aside all the documents that were marked as
exhibits during the trial and, merely on the basis of the statement
made by the learned counsel for the Insurance Company that no post-
mortem examination had been conducted, allowed the appeal.
6. In the considered opinion of this Court, the Tribunal passed a
well reasoned order awarding a sum of Rs.18,25,000/- to the
claimants. In view thereof, the impugned judgment passed by the
High Court deserves to be set aside, and it is accordingly set
aside. The claimants shall be entitled to the awarded amount along
with interest at the rate of 12% per annum from the date of the
petition till realization.
7. With the above observations and directions, the Special Leave
PETITION(S) FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C) NO(S).  17375/2025

--- Page 241 ---
4
Petition stands disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
…………………………………………………………………J.
[ SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA ]
…………………………………………………………………J.
[ NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH ]

PETITION(S) FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C) NO(S).  17375/2025

--- Page 243 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.       OF 2026
(arising out of SLP(C) No.20672/2022)
A. SUBRAMANIAN ..... APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
M.K. VEDHAMBAL & ORS.  ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. The appellant, A. Subramanian, is aggrieved by the order dated
16.09.2022 in C.R.P. No. 1968/2014, passed by a learned Judge
of the High Court of Judicature at Madras under Article 227 of
the Constitution of India1. 
3. By the said order, the learned Judge held that the revision
filed by the respondent under Article 227 of the Constitution
was maintainable against the order passed by the trial court
rejecting the plaint under Order VII Rule 11 of the Code of
Civil  Procedure,  19082,  at  the  behest  of  the  defendant
therein, the appellant.  
4. By order dated 21.11.2022, this Court stayed the operation of
the impugned order. 
5. Having heard the learned counsel for the parties, we are of
the opinion that the learned Judge was not justified in
1  For short “Constitution”
2  For short “CPC”
1

--- Page 244 ---
entertaining  the  petition  filed  under  Article  227  of
Constitution on the given facts. Though there can be no fetter
on the jurisdiction of the High Court to exercise power under
Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution, the discretion to do
so would be conditioned by the factual circumstances, which
have  to  warrant  such  exercise  overlooking  the  ordinary
procedure that may be applicable in a given case.  
6. In the case on hand, the respondent had filed the suit before
the learned XV Assistant Judge, City Civil Court at Chennai,
in which the appellant, being the defendant therein, filed an
application for rejection under Order VII Rule 11 of the CPC.
The said application was accepted by the trial court and by
order dated 23.10.2013, the trial court rejected the plaint. 
7. We are informed that, apart from filing the subject petition
before the High Court under Article 227 of the Constitution,
the respondent also instituted a Regular First Appeal against
the rejection of her plaint by the trial court. 
8. The matter came before this Court earlier in the context of
the maintainability of the subject revision and, by order
dated  16.07.2014,  this  Court  noted  that  a  miscellaneous
petition had been filed by the appellant in the said revision
petition raising objection as to its maintainability and that
the  said  miscellaneous  petition  was  still  pending
consideration. This Court, accordingly, requested the High
Court to take up the said miscellaneous petition for hearing,
making it clear that no opinion had been expressed on merits. 
2

--- Page 245 ---
9. It is on the strength of this direction that the miscellaneous
petition filed by the appellant was taken up by the High
Court, resulting in the impugned order dated 16.09.2022. 
10. We find that the High Court was of the opinion that, as the
issue, on the strength of which the trial court had rejected
the plaint, stood settled by way of a judgment of this Court,
it was a fit case to entertain the revision under Article 227
of Constitution. It is on this basis that the revision was
allowed restoring the suit to the file of the trial Court. 
11. We may, however, note that this ground could, as well, have
been raised by the respondent in the first appeal filed
against the rejection of the plaint. Unless extraordinary
circumstances  exist,  that  warrant  deviation  from  the
prescribed  procedure  laid  down  by  law  in  relation  to
challenging an order, the High Court would not be justified in
entertaining a petition filed under Articles 226 or 227 of
Constitution.  The  remedies  provided  by  law  cannot  be
circumvented without just cause.
12. In the case on hand, we find that no such exceptional
circumstances were made out, warranting the revision petition
filed by the respondent being entertained. 
13. We, accordingly, set aside the impugned order dated 16.09.2022
passed by the High Court and the revision filed by the
respondent against the rejection of her plaint shall stand
dismissed  as  not  maintainable.  As  noted  hereinbefore,  a
regular  First  Appeal  has  already  been  preferred  by  the
3

--- Page 246 ---
respondent against the rejection of her plaint by the trial
court, it is for the respondent to pursue the same in
accordance with law and due procedure.  Leaving it open to the
respondent to do so, the appeal is allowed.
14.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
.....................J.
(SANJAY KUMAR)
.....................J.
(SANJEEV SACHDEVA)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 22, 2026.
4

--- Page 247 ---

5

--- Page 248 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.           OF 2026
(@SLP(CRL.) NO.9369 OF 2026)
MD. AASHIK @ MOHAMMAD ASHIK ..... APPELLANT(S)
              VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant has been arraigned as an accused in connection
with PS Case No. 237/2024 whereunder it has been alleged
that appellant possessed 960 bottles of Codeine Cough
Syrup, which is a prohibited drug under the  Narcotic Drugs
and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 (NDPS Act)  and as
such the FIR came to be registered against the appellant
and his mother for the offences punishable under Sections
8(c), 21(c), 25 and 29 of the NDPS Act.
4. Appellant has surrendered before the trial court on
17.09.2025 and has remained in custody till date. On
conclusion of the investigation the jurisdictional police
have filed the chargesheet and even the jurisdictional
court has also framed the charge and trial is yet to
commence. The application filed before the trial court for
grant of regular bail, has been rejected and same has been
1                                  SLP(CRL.) NO.9369 OF 2026

--- Page 249 ---
affirmed by the High Court. Hence, this appeal.
5.  Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  appellant  would
contend that alleged prohibited material which has been
seized, is within the permissible limit. It is also his
contention that appellant was not apprehended from the
scene of the offence and no recovery was made from his
conscious possession and claiming that the vehicle from
where the prohibited material was recovered does not belong
to him and appellant is not the registered owner. Hence, he
seeks for appellant being enlarged on bail. Per contra, the
learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State would
vehemently  oppose  the  grant  of  bail  contending  that
vehicle from which the prohibited material has been seized,
was parked in the appellant’s house and as such he is
deemed to be in conscious possession of the said material.
Hence, he prays for rejection of the prayer.
6. This Court having heard the learned counsels appearing
for the parties and on perusal of the records and after
bestowing our attention to the rival contentions raised at
the Bar, we notice that undisputedly the vehicle from which
the material has been recovered, did not prima facie belong
to the appellant. As to how the said vehicle was parked in
the house of the appellant and as to who had stored the
said prohibited material, is an issue which has to be
thrashed out after evaluating the evidence that may be
tendered  by  the  prosecution.  Hence,  taking  into
consideration that the investigation has been conclcuded,
chargesheet has been filed and his continued custody would
not be warranted, we are of the considered view that
appellant is entitled for regular bail. In order to allay
2                                  SLP(CRL.) NO.9369 OF 2026

--- Page 250 ---
the apprehension of the prosecution, it is  necessary to
impose stringent conditions by the trial court.
7. Accordingly, appeal is allowed. Impugned order is set
aside. Appellant is ordered to be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as the jurisdictional court may deem
fit to impose and also on the condition that appellant
should appear before the jurisdictional trial court on all
dates of hearing except when exempted for any specific
reason.
8. It is also made clear that in the event of any of the
conditions imposed by the trial court or by this Court,
being violated, the respondent-prosecution would be at
liberty to seek for cancellation of the bail granted by
this Court by filing an appropriate application before the
jurisdictional trial court, which shall be adjudicated on
its merits.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(V. MOHANA)
New Delhi;
July 21, 2026.
3                                  SLP(CRL.) NO.9369 OF 2026

O R D E R
1. During pendency of the present transfer petition,
the parties have settled their disputes and have moved a
joint application under Article 142 of the Constitution of
India, for passing appropriate orders including a decree of
divorce,  on  the  ground  of  mutual  consent  due  to  the
marriage having been broken down irretrievably.
2. The following are the terms and conditions of the
settlement as mentioned in paragraph Nos.5 and 6 of the IA
No. 202757/2026:
“5. That after filling the present petition,
both the parties have settled their dispute
once for all on the following terms and
conditions:-
a. It is agreed that both the parties
will make a joint request to this Hon’ble
Court to dissolve their marriage on the
grounds  of  irretrievable  breakdown  of
marriage alternatively on the grounds of
mutual consent in terms of Section 13 (B)
1

--- Page 254 ---
of Hindu Marriage Act 1955. 
b. It is agreed that Respondent shall pay
a sum of Rs. 2200000/- (Twenty-Two Lacs
Only) by way of demand draft to the
Petitioner towards stridhan, maintenance
and  permanent  alimony  for  the
past/present and future. 
c. It is agreed that both the parties
shall make a joint request to dispose off
the aforesaid cases in terms of their
compromise/settlement  mentioned  in  the
present application before this Hon’ble
Court.
d. It is agreed that both the parties
shall  not  file  RESPONDENT(S)  any
cases/complaints  against  each
other/against any 3 family members in
court or at any other forum arising out
of the present matrimonial dispute and
shall  not  pursue  any  of  the
cases/complaints  pending  against  each
other anywhere in India and Canada, even
if the same is not specifically stated or
mentioned herein.

disputes (past, present and future) and
the respondent has agreed to pay a one-
time  permanent  alimony  of  Rs.22  Lacs
(Twenty Two Lacs Only) to the petitioner
by way of demand draft No. 635768 drawn
on Yes Bank Ltd. dated 15.05.2026 towards
full and final settlement for maintenance
(past, present, and future), permanent
alimony, jewelry, other article, movable
and immovable properties etc. and the
petitioner undertakes not to put forth
any further claim towards the same in
future at any stage. 
f. That it is agreed that the parties
shall not interfere in the peaceful life
of each other in any manner whatsoever.
2

--- Page 255 ---
They shall not have any communication
with each other by themselves, or through
their  relatives,  after  the  grant  of

communication  such  as  SMS,  Whatsapp,
Email, post etc.  
6. Therefore, it is clear that the marriage
has irretrievably been broken down with no
possibility of reconciliation. Hence, it is a
fit  case  where  this  Hon’ble  Court  may
exercise its extraordinary powers to grant a
decree of divorce.”
3. The demand draft as mentioned in paragraph 5(e) has
been  handed  over  by  the  respondent’s  counsel  to  the
petitioner’s counsel in the Court today.
4. Considering the settlement and the marriage between
the parties having been broken down irretrievably, we are
inclined to allow IA No. 202757/2026 and direct that
marriage between the parties solemnized on 12.12.2013 stand
dissolved by a decree of divorce by mutual consent.

the ground that he was carrying 40 bottles of
local wine ‘Gulab label’ and it was recovered
from him on 23.11.2007. After trial, appellant
was convicted under Section 60 of the Excise Act
and sentenced to six months simple imprisonment
with  fine  of  Rs.6,000/-.  The  appeal  filed
1

--- Page 259 ---
challenging the same was dismissed vide order
dated 10.09.2013 and Criminal Revision Petition
No.282/2013 challenging appellate court’s order
came  to  be  partly  allowed  and  sentence  was
modified  to  the  period  of  11  days  already
undergone  and  the  fine  was  enhanced  to
Rs.25,000/- which was subject to payment of the
said amount within two months with a default
sentence  that  he  would  undergo  the  sentence
imposed  by  the  trial  court.  On  account  of
appellant  having  failed  to  deposit  the  fine
amount, Application being MCRC No.4681/2026 was
filed before the High Court seeking extension of
time by 15 days which was also dismissed on
15.06.2026. Hence, this appeal.
5. Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
has drawn the attention of the Court to the
affidavit of Shri Aditya Singh Chuahan enclosing
therewith  receipt  No.496694  dated  17.07.2026
issued by the office of Additional Chief Judicial
Magistrate, Rishikesh. It would clearly emerge
2

--- Page 260 ---
from  the  affidavit  as  well  as  the  receipt
enclosed thereto that fine amount of Rs.25,000/-
has been deposited on 17.07.2026.
6. Thus, the order of the Revisional Court
stands complied. Hence, continuation of appellant
in jail is not warranted. He is ordered to be
released forthwith, if not required in any other
case. Accordingly, the Appeal is allowed.

disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
Transfer Petition (Civil) No. 1120 of 2025
Rekha Devi
...Petitioner 
Versus
Mahesh Kumar
...Respondent
O R D E R 
Marital  discord  has  separated  the  petitioner  -wife
and the respondent-husband. The two children born in the
wedlock are with the respondent-husband; as noticed by
this  Court  in  order  dated  17.04.2026  with  a  total
disconnect from their mother, the petitioner.
2. There are various cases pending against each other
and  attempts  to  mediation  having  failed,  this  Court
intervened and arrived at an amicable settlement i.e. final
separation  on  payment  of  Rs.  17  lakhs  (Rupees
Seventeen Lakhs). The terms of the settlement have been
reduced to writing in the application filed under Article142
of the Constitution of India for divorce on the ground of
irretrievable break down of marriage. The learned Counsel
Page 1 of 4
TP(C) No. 1120 of 2025 

--- Page 264 ---
for  the  petitioner,  in  the  presence  of  the  petitioner,
confirms that the entire amount of alimony of Rs. 17 lakhs
have been received. The terms of the settlement indicate
all the following cases pending between each other would
be withdrawn.
3. As of now the petitioner has filed two cases and one
complaint which are as under:
I. Under Protection of Women from Domestic Violence
Act, 2005 with an application under Section 23 for
grant of interim and ex-parte orders registered as
MC No. 02/2025 titled ‘Smt. Rekha vs. Mahesh
Kumar  &  Anr. ’  before  the  Chief  Judicial
Magistrate,  South-West  District,  Dwarka
District Court, New Delhi.
II. FIR No. 215 of 2025  filed by the petitioner against
the  respondent  husband,  at  Police  Station

the Indian Penal Code, 1860.

his sister.
Page 2 of 4
TP(C) No. 1120 of 2025 

--- Page 265 ---
4. The two cases referred to above, and the complaint
shall stand withdrawn on production of this order before
the competent court/authority.
5. The respondent husband has also filed FIR No. 363 of
2024 against the petitioner at Police Station Mundawar
under Sections 137(2), 87 and 303(2) of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. It has been agreed upon that the
petitioner wife would execute necessary documents and
cooperate with the police for release of certain articles,
which is the subject matter of this FIR. The Station House
Officer shall depute suitable police personnel to carry out
release of the articles and  to  ensure  execution  of  the
documents, the inventory of which shall be supplied to
the respondent husband and kept in the files of the SHO
of the police station.
6. The children having lost contact with the mother, it
has been agreed that she would be permitted to have
video calls at regular intervals, at least twice a month and
meeting  would  be  facilitated  on  mutually  convenient
times at the place of residence of the children, at the
Page 3 of 4
TP(C) No. 1120 of 2025 

--- Page 266 ---
option of the children, who will remain in the custody of
the father, the respondent. 
7. The  above  terms  are  recorded  and  finding  the
marriage to have irretrievably broken down, we invoke
our powers under Article 142 of the Constitution of India
and grant a decree of divorce to the parties, dissolving
their marriage.  
8. The H.M.A. Petition No. 240/109 of 2024 titled
‘Mahesh Kumar vs. Rekha Devi’  pending before the
Additional District Judge, Mundawar, Alwar District
Court, Rajasthan, shall hence be disposed of based on

9. The above petition is disposed of in the aforestated
terms. 
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

--- Page 268 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).  3291/2026
      @SLP(Crl) No.9765/2026
PRATAP                                             Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH                         Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 06.05.2026 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Allahabad rejecting the
application of the appellant seeking suspension of sentence
pending consideration of his appeal. 
4. At the outset, learned counsel for the appellant submits
that the appeal against the order of conviction is of the
year 2022 and there is no likelihood of the appeal being
heard in near future. Besides, it is an admitted position
that appellant has remained in jail in connection with the
present case for a period of 14 years. In such circumstances,
it has been submitted that the appellant may be released on
bail after suspension of sentence pending consideration of
1

--- Page 269 ---
his appeal.
5. In response to the aforesaid submission, the learned
counsel for the State submits that appellant has criminal
antecedents. The details of those antecedents are provided in
paragraph 20 of the counter affidavit.
6. A perusal of paragraph 20 of the counter affidavit would
reveal that the cases which are enlisted there except one are
all of a period prior to 2012. The case in which he has been
convicted is of 2012. The other case, which is after 2012, is
a case of 2023 in which the appellant has been implicated
with the aid of Section 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860
(“IPC”).
7. The submission of the learned counsel for the appellant
is that he has been falsely implicated in the case of the
year 2023 as would be clear from the fact that during that
period, the appellant was incarcerated in jail.
8. Having regard to the fact that the appellant has by now
suffered 14 years of incarceration since the date of his
arrest, and the appeal of the year 2022 is not likely to be
heard in near future, the very purpose of the appeal would
stand frustrated if the appellant is not released on bail at
this stage.
2

--- Page 270 ---
9. In such circumstances, we deem it appropriate to allow
this appeal. The order dated 06.05.2026 passed by the High
Court rejecting the prayer of the appellant to suspend the
sentence pending consideration of his appeal by the High
Court is set aside. The sentence awarded by the trial court
to the appellant shall remain suspended pending consideration
of the appeal by the High Court.
10. The appellant shall be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the trial court may deem fit to impose in the
facts and circumstances of the case.
11. It is made clear that we have not expressed any opinion
on the merits of the case pending before the High Court. 
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
…………………………………………….J
     [MANOJ MISRA]
…………………………………………….J
[VIJAY BISHNOI]
New Delhi 
July 21, 2026
3

--- Page 271 ---

CIVIL APPEAL NO.9202 OF 2026
[Arising out of SLP(C) No.468/2026]
M/S DEVIS SURGICO APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
CONTEMPT PETITION(C) NO.175/2026
IN 
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION(C) NO.468/2026
O R D E R
C.A. No.9202/2026 @ SLP(C) No.468/2026
1. Leave granted.
2. After hearing the learned counsel appearing for the
parties briefly, it appears to us that in the interest
of justice and equity, the matter requires to be heard
and  decided  by  the  High  Court  afresh.  We  are  not
assigning reasons and/or noticing the facts so that any
prejudice is caused to the parties.
3. We may, however, hasten to add that, according to
respondents, the matter ought to be referred to the
National Green Tribunal instead of the High Court. Such
objection, in our considered opinion, can be raised and
shall be considered by the High Court. We do not express
any opinion in relation thereto.
4. For the reasons aforesaid, the appeal is allowed.
The impugned judgment dated 10.12.2025 is set aside, and
the matter is remitted to the High Court for fresh
1

--- Page 282 ---
adjudication.
5. The parties are directed to appear before the High
Court on 31.07.2025.
Contempt Petition(C) No.175/2026
6. In view of the order passed in C.A. No.9202/2026,
nothing  survives  in  this  petition.  The  same  is,
accordingly, disposed of.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
M.A. No. 1122/2026 and M.A. Diary No.18445/2026
1. Delay in filing M.A. Diary No.18445/2026 is
condoned.
2. The parties in Civil Appeal No. 1611 of 2026
are the applicants in the captioned miscellaneous
applications.
3. The Court, with a view, to resolving the
disputes  between  the  parties,  has  spent  a
substantial amount of time.
4. The parties could not come to a mutually
acceptable settlement of their disputes.
5. The prayers in the applications, though,
have  been  prefaced  as  clarificatory;  after
appreciating the order in its entirety, we are of
the  view  that  the  prayers,  as  made,  are  not
entertained by way of the present miscellaneous
applications.
6. Therefore,  both  the  miscellaneous
applications are dismissed.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

   UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                    O R D E R
SLP(Crl) No. 10684/2026 
Heard learned counsel for the parties
and perused the material placed on record.
Considering the facts and circumstances
of the case, the period of incarceration
already undergone by the petitioner which is
more than five years, we are inclined to
suspend the sentence of the  petitioner and
grant bail to the petitioner during the
pendency  of  the  appeal  before  the  High
Court.
Accordingly,  we  direct  that  the
petitioner be released on bail, on such
terms and conditions as may be imposed by
the Trial Court, in connection with P.S.
  SLP (Crl.)  No(s).  10684/2026 etc.                 2 

--- Page 295 ---
case No. 5 of 2021 Thana, N.C.B.(Government
Official), District Patna, Bihar.
The Special Leave Petition and pending
applications are disposed of accordingly.
SLP(Crl) No. 10686/2026 
 Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner states that the petitioner has
already undergone approximately 4½ years of
incarceration.
 Ms.  Archana  Pathak  Dave,  learned
Additional Solicitor General, objects to
the said statement and submits that the
petitioner  has  undergone  less  that  one
year of incarceration.
  We have perused the order dated 18 th
December, 2024, whereby bail was granted to
the petitioner during the pendency of the
trial, The said order records that the
petitioner had been in custody since 27 th
  SLP (Crl.)  No(s).  10684/2026 etc.                 3 

--- Page 296 ---
February, 2021.
However, the period of custody from 27 th
February, 2021 to 18th December, 2024 is not
reflected in the custody certificate filed
by the petitioner.
At the request of Ms.  Archana Pathak
Dave, learned Additional Solicitor General,
list  this matter again on 27 th July, 2026,
to  enable  her  to  obtain  further
instructions.
(SONIA BHASIN)
ASSISTANT REGISTRAR-CUM-PS
(RANJANA SHAILEY)
ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
  SLP (Crl.)  No(s).  10684/2026 etc.                 4

 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
 CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL No.________ OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(CIVIL) No.5269/2026)
KARAM SINGH                                        Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
PAL SINGH & ORS.                                   Respondent(s)
     O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Despite non service of notice on respondent
no.1,  we  proceed  to  take  up  the  matter  for
disposal as respondent no.1 was the driver of the
offending vehicle and respondent nos. 2 and 3 have
not entered appearance despite service of notice.
3. In  a  road  accident  which  occurred  on
09.03.2013, the appellant has suffered amputation
of his right leg resulting in 80 per cent of
permanent disability. However, the Motor Accident
Claims  Tribunal  (for  short,  ‘the  MACT’)  has
awarded compensation of Rs.4,93,689/- along with
interest  at  the  rate  of  6%  per  annum.  The
appellant’s  appeal  for  enhancement  has  been
dismissed  by  the  High  Court  for  want  of
prosecution  as  appellant’s  lawyer  repeatedly

--- Page 302 ---
2
failed to appear before the Court and appellant’s
prayer for restoration of the said order has also
been dismissed.
4. Upon hearing learned counsel for the parties
and in the interest of justice, we direct that the
appellant’s  appeal  before  the  High  Court  be
restored to file to its original number and the
same shall be decided expeditiously, in accordance
with law.
5. Accordingly, the appeal stands disposed of.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall also
stand disposed of.
.....................J.
    (PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA)
....................J.
(SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR)
New Delhi
20-07-2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  (NO).3345/2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.11073/2026]
DHARAM CHAND                                      APPELLANT(S)
                       VERSUS
CENTRAL BUREAU OF INVESTIGATION                  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 31.10.2025 passed
by the High Court of Himachal Pradesh at Shimla whereby
the prayer of the appellant seeking suspension of sentence
awarded by the Trial Court has been rejected.
4. The submission on behalf of the appellant is that the
sentence awarded by the trial court for various offences
would run concurrently, and therefore, the sentence is of
4 years whereas the appellant has already served about one
year in jail and the appeal being of the year 2025 is not
Crl. A @ SLP (Crl.) No.11073/2026                                                       1

--- Page 310 ---
likely to be heard in near future. 
5. Having regard to the sentence awarded by the trial court
and there being no likelihood of the appeal being decided
in near future, without expressing any opinion on merits
of the pending appeal, we are of the view that the
appellant is entitled for suspension of sentence, pending
consideration of his appeal by the High Court.
6. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed. The order passed by
the High Court rejecting the bail prayer of the appellant
is set aside.
7. The appellant shall be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the trial court may deem fit to impose in
the facts and circumstances of the case.
8. All pending applications shall stand disposed of.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C)  NO(S).  27031-27032/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  20-03-2025
in DBSAW No. 731/2023 23-05-2025 in DBRPW No. 103/2025 passed by
the High Court of Judicature for Rajasthan at Jaipur]
RISHI KESH MEENA                                   Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ORS.                          Respondent(s)
IA No. 51196/2026 - APPLICATION FOR PERMISSION
IA No. 222352/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Sunil Kumar Verma, AOR
                   Mohd. Wasay Khan, Adv.
                   Mr. Desam Sudhakara Reddy, Adv.
                   Mr. Himank Nargotra, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Shiv Mangal Sharma, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Saurabh Rajpal, Adv.
                   Ms. Sonali Gaur, Adv.
                   Ms. Nidhi Jaswal, AOR
                                      
                   Mr. Yuvraj Samant, Adv.
                   Ms. Neha Amola, Adv.
                   Mr. Rajesh Singh Chauhan, AOR                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The submission on behalf of the learned counsel for
the petitioner is that neither the Service Rules, 1979 nor
the Recruitment Advertisement provided that work experience
1

--- Page 314 ---
in  a  State  Forensic  Laboratory  or  Central  Forensic
Laboratory or in a Public Sector Unit would have to be in
the field/division concerned.  In such circumstances, when
it  is  the  admitted  case  of  the  respondents  that  the
petitioner had obtained marks above the cut off fixed for
the post of Senior Scientific Officer (Toxicology) and
possess the minimum academic qualification specified in the
Rules as well as in the Recruitment Notice, he could not
have been denied appointment on the ground that his work
experience in the Forensic Science laboratory was not in the
concerned  division.  In  support  of  his  submission,  the
learned counsel for the petitioner has placed before us the
relevant Rules as well as the Schedule to the Rules and the
Recruitment Notice.
2. The Recruitment Notice for the purpose of eligibility
to  the  post  of  Senior  Scientific  Officer  (Toxicology)
mentions essential qualification “M.Sc IInd division in
Chemistry from a University established by Law in India; and
(b) Two year’s experience of working in a Forensic Science
Laboratory of a State or Central Government or similar
experience in Public Undertaking or Corporation.
3. Prima  facie,  it  appears,  there  is  no  specific
requirement of experience in a particular division of the
Forensic  Science  Laboratory  of  a  State  or  Central
Government.
4. The  learned  counsel  representing  the  respondent
submits that both qualifications are provided under one
2

--- Page 315 ---
heading in the Schedule to the Rules.  The first part
relates to educational qualification and the second part
relates to experience in a Forensic Science Laboratory.  In
such circumstances, it would be deemed that the experience
must relate to the field in respect of which educational
qualification  is  required.   Accordingly,  it  has  been
submitted on behalf of the respondents that the Recruiting
Department had issued a clarificatory letter to the Public
Service Commission stating therein that experience in the
concerned division would alone be considered and not general
experience in the Forensic Science Laboratory.
5. At this stage, the learned counsel for the petitioner
placed before us the certificate issued by Central Forensic
Science Laboratory, Central Bureau of Investigation, New
Delhi regarding the work experience of the petitioner.
Relying on that certificate, the learned counsel for the
petitioner submits that the work experience certificate does
not reflect work in any particular division though it
reflects experience of working on a specified post.  In such
circumstances, it has been submitted that there was no
requirement of work in any particular division of the
Forensic Science Laboratory; the work experience would have
to relate only to the number of years in a particular
Forensic Science Laboratory, which the petitioner fulfils.
6. Having regard to the above submissions, before we
finally decide the matter, we need clarification on two
aspects:
3

--- Page 316 ---
(i) Whether the posts advertised, in respect of which the
petitioner had applied and found above the cutoff for
selection, are still vacant or have been filled up?;
(ii) Whether Forensic Science Laboratory of the State and
the Central Government have separate divisions. If so,
whether Forensic Science Laboratories issue certificate of
work experience in a particular division or on a post?
7. List these matters for further hearing on 12.08.2026. 
  (KAVITA PAHUJA)                               (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 317 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
Transfer Petition(s)(Criminal)  No(s).  1178/2025
ANKIT MATHUR                                  Petitioner(s)
                           VERSUS
DURGESH NANDNI & ORS.                         Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties.  The parties,
namely, Ankit Mathur and Durgesh Nandini, are also
present in-person. 
2. Through this petition, the petitioner (Husband) had
sought for transfer of proceedings initiated by the
first respondent under Section 12 of the Protection of
Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 bearing CC
No.1957/2023 pending before Metropolitan Magistrate,
Mahila Court, Patiala House, New Delhi.
3. During these proceedings, the parties were referred to
the  Mediation  Center  of  this  Court  to  explore
possibility of settlement. 
4. Pursuant to the aforesaid order, parties participated
in mediation and arrived at a mediated settlement. Now
1

--- Page 318 ---
a joint application has been submitted, duly signed by
the parties and their respective counsels, annexing
therewith  a  Settlement  Agreement  arrived  during
mediation  proceedings  before  the  Supreme  Court
Mediation Centre. The Settlement Agreement has been
drawn on 07.04.2026, signed by the parties and their
respective counsels on 09.04.2026.
5. The terms and conditions of the mediated agreement are
incorporated in the paragraphs (v) and (vi) thereof.
6. It is not in dispute between parties that they have
fulfilled  their  mutual  obligations  under  the
settlement, and no further action is required under
the settlement except that a decree of divorce by
mutual consent is to be drawn.
7. For  that  end,  the  parties  have  invoked  the
jurisdiction of this Court under Article 142 of the
Constitution of India for passing a decree of divorce
by mutual consent in terms of the Settlement Deed
dated 07.04.2026 (verified on 09.04.2026).
8. Having regard to the fact that parties have resolved
their differences by mutual agreement, we deem it
appropriate to invoke our powers under Section 142 of
the Constitution of India to put a quietus to the
2

--- Page 319 ---
litigation between the parties.
9. Accordingly, this transfer petition is disposed of in
terms of the Settlement. Registry shall draw a decree
of divorce between the parties by mutual consent. The
settlement agreement shall be part of the decree. 
10. The transfer petition and all other applications shall
stand disposed of in terms above.

ITEM NO.14                  COURT NO.10                 SECTION XIV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No.27326/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 23-04-2024 
in WPC No. 5561/2024 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New 
Delhi]
MOOL CHAND VERMA                                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                                 Respondent(s)
IA No. 204216/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 204219/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 22-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Himanshu Gautam, Adv.
                   Mr. Harshit, Adv.
                   Ms. Niharika Punn, Adv.
                   Mr. Lokesh Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Shivam Shivhare, Adv.
                   Ms. Neha Rai, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Anil Kaushik, A.S.G.
Mr. Devraj Bhattacharjee, Adv.
Ms. Shambhavi Sharma, Adv.
Mr. Sarthak Karol, Adv.
Mr. Gyanendra Singh, Adv.
Mr. Yogesh Vats, Adv.
                   Dr. N. Visakamurthy, AOR
                   
                   Mr. Arvind Kumar Sharma, AOR
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
2. This petition seeks leave to impugn the judgment and
order of the High Court of Delhi at New Delhi dated
1

--- Page 330 ---
23.04.2024 passed in W.P. (C) No.5561/2024.
3. Relevant facts are as under: Upon appraisal of service
records, batchmates of the petitioner were promoted in the
year 2008 whereas the petitioner came to be promoted four
years later i.e., in the year 2012. Later, in the year
2023, petitioner made a prayer for supply of his service
entries made prior to the year 2008. On the said prayer
those entries were provided to the petitioner. On finding
that those entries rated the petitioner as "average", by
relying on this Court’s decision in Dev Dutt vs. Union of
India  &  Ors.  (2008)  8SCC  725,  a  representation  was
submitted for upgradation of those entries by claiming
that those were not communicated earlier. However, the
said  representation  was  rejected  vide  order  dated
20.02.2024.
4. Aggrieved by rejection of the request to upgrade those
entries, the petitioner filed writ petition before the
High Court seeking (a) quashing of the order by which his
representation was rejected; and (b) upgradation of those
entries i.e., entries prior to the year 2007.
5. The High Court dismissed the writ petition on ground of
latches  by  noticing  that  petitioner’s  batchmates  were
promoted  in  the  year  2008;  the  petitioner  raised  no
grievance then; and thereafter, in 2012, the petitioner
himself was promoted, which he accepted without protest. 
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
decision of this Court in Dev Dutt (supra) has been
2

--- Page 331 ---
affirmed in Abhijit Ghosh Dastidar vs. Union of India &
Ors. (2009) 16 SCC 146 and, thereafter, has again been
affirmed in Sukhdev Singh vs. Union of India & Ors. (2013)
9 SCC 566. In such circumstances, the department was under
an  obligation  to  communicate  all  entries  below  the
benchmark entry to the petitioner. As those entries were
not communicated earlier, the cause of action arose as and
when the entries were communicated and again when the
petitioner’s representation was rejected. Therefore, the
writ petition cannot be held barred by laches. 
7. In response to the above submissions, the learned ASG,
representing the respondent, submitted that having not
questioned the promotion of his batch-mates or denial of
his own promotion in the year 2008, and having accepted
his own promotion in 2012 without a demur, when the law in
respect of communication of entries stood settled in the
year 2008, the petitioner had lost his right to question
those entries, therefore, the High Court rightly dismissed
the  petition  for  latches.  Besides,  we  were  informed,
petitioner's future promotions would be considered basis
entries  of  last  5  years  preceding  consideration,  and
therefore, entries prior to 2008 are not relevant at all.
8. In response, on behalf of petitioner it was submitted
that the petitioner had questioned not denial of promotion
in  the  year  2008,  but  the  order  rejecting  his
representation,  in  the  writ  petition,  therefore  the
petition ought not to have been dismissed on ground of
3

--- Page 332 ---
laches.  
9. We have considered the rival contentions. In our view,
since  the  petitioner  had  not  questioned  denial  of
promotion in the year 2008 i.e., when his batchmates were
promoted,  and  even  now  he  has  sought  no  relief  of
promotion with effect from 2008, taking into consideration
that his future promotion shall be governed by entries of
preceding 5 years, we are of the view that entertaining
this petition would be an exercise in futility.
10. In such circumstances, we decline to entertain this
petition and the same is, accordingly, dismissed.
11. Pending application(s), shall stand disposed of.
(KRITIKA TIWARI)                                (SAPNA BANSAL)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 333 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 3339     OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.11331/2026)
ANKIT JOHRI & ANR.      APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF U.P.          RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1. We have heard learned counsel for the appellant(s)
and learned counsel for the respondent-State of Uttar
Pradesh.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant(s) have been arrested in connection with
Case Crime No.296 of 2025 registered at Police Station-
Jaswant  Nagar,  District-  Etawah,  Uttar  Pradesh  under
Sections 61(2), 3(5), 340(2), 336(3), 338 and 318(4) of
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (in short ‘BNS’).
4. Allegation against the appellant(s) is that they had
created fictitious supply invoices and thereby enabled
availing of fake input tax credit.
5. Appellant(s) were arrested on 03.02.2026 and since
then, they are in custody. The High Court, vide the
1

--- Page 334 ---
impugned order dated 16.04.2026, declined to release the
appellant(s) on bail.
6. Aggrieved thereby, the related Special Leave Petition
came to be filed. This Court vide order dated 18.06.2026
had issued notice.
7. One of the contentions raised before the High Court
on behalf of the appellant(s) was that the accusations
against the appellant(s) falls squarely within the realm
of Section 132 of the Uttar Pradesh Goods and Services Tax
Act, 2017. If that is the position, then the provisions of
Sections 69, 132, 134, 135 and 138 of the said Act would
be attracted. Consequently, provisions of the BNS cannot
be invoked.
8. The  High  Court  did  not  accept  the  aforesaid
contention.
9. In the hearing today, while learned counsel for the
appellant(s) sought for bail on the ground that they have
already served more than five months of incarceration with
investigation complete and charge sheet filed, therefore
bail should be granted to them, learned counsel for the
respondent-State of Uttar Pradesh submitted that the Court
may consider passing appropriate order.
10. We leave this question open to be taken up in an
appropriate proceeding. The question is when Sections 69,
132, 134, 135 and 138 of the Uttar Pradesh Goods and
Services Tax Act, 2017 or for that matter, the Central
Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 lay down a particular
2

--- Page 335 ---
procedure for arrest and prosecution, the same being a
special legislation, can an accused be proceeded against
under provisions of a general law, be it the Indian Penal
Code, 1860 or the BNS. We leave this aspect to be
considered in an appropriate proceeding.
11. Insofar as the present case is concerned, having
regard to the period of detention undergone and the fact
that the investigation is complete, the impugned order
dated 16.04.2026 passed by the High Court is set aside.
12. Consequently, we direct that the appellant(s) shall
be produced before the concerned Magistrate within a
maximum period of 07 days from today, whereafter they
shall be released on bail on such terms and conditions as
may be deemed appropriate.
13. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeal is allowed.
14. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
22th JULY, 2026.
AK

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 11603 of 2026)
SALMAN         APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Appellant is convicted for committing offence under
Section 8(c) read with Section 20 (b)(ii)(b) of Narcotic
Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 (for short
‘NDPS Act’) for having found in possession of 1.620 kgms of
Ganja. The Trial Court sentenced him to undergo rigorous
imprisonment for four years and a fine of Rs.10,000/-.
3. In  his  pending  appeal  before  the  High  Court,
appellant moved an application for suspension of sentence
which has been dismissed by the High Court, on the ground
that the appellant has previous antecedents of committing
offences including under NDPS Act and MP Excise Act, 1915.
4. In this appeal, appellant’s father has filed an
affidavit explaining the antecedents. It appears in the
offence under Section 307 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860
1

--- Page 338 ---
appellant has already been acquitted and the remaining four
offences are under the MP Excise Act. In the other case,
under the NDPS Act, appellant was found in possession of
700 gms of Ganja (small quantity). In the present case,
appellant is in jail for more than one year and five
months.
5. Although, ordinarily suspension of sentence is not
allowed when the convict is a habitual offender and is
found to have committed offences similar in nature under
the NDPS Act, but present is a case where the appeal
pending before the High Court is of the year 2025 and
appellant is suffering imprisonment for four years only.
6. While  rejecting  his  prayer  for  suspension  of
sentence, the High Court has not posted the appeal itself
for final hearing. Thus, considering that the disposal of
the appeal pending before the High Court may take time and
eventually when the appeal is heard, appellant may have
undergone the entire sentence imposed upon him, we are of
the view that the sentence imposed upon the appellant
should be suspended during pendency of the appeal before
the High Court.
7. Accordingly, we allow the appeal, set aside the
impugned order and direct that the appellant be released on
bail, pending his appeal before the High Court concerning
crime no. 382/2023 registered at Police Station Rajpur,
District Barwani, subject to such terms and conditions as
may be imposed by the Trial Court.
2

--- Page 339 ---
8. In  addition,  appellant  shall  appear  before  the
concerned Police Station on first Monday of every month
(between 11:00 am to 12:00 noon), and shall not commit any
other crime during pendency of the appeal before the High
Court.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  
 ………………………………………………………J.
             [PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA]
   
    ………………………………………………………J.
           [SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR]
NEW DELHI;
July 21, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
I.A. No.180843 of 2026 & I.A. No. 200247 of 2026
IN
Civil Appeal No.9735 of 2024
Axis Bank Limited
…Appellant
Versus
Majestic Auto Limited & Anr.                                             
…Respondents
O R D E R
The  applicant  in  I.A.  No.180843/2026  has  sought  for
substitution of itself as the appellant, the applicant having obtained
assignment of the debt, which is relatable to the subject matter of
the impugned order, being successful in a public auction where the
appellant’s bid came successful.
2. The application is filed by the learned counsel appearing for the
original  appellant  and  the  one  who  seeks  substitution.  I.A.
No.180843 of 2026 hence stands allowed.  The registry is directed to
amend the cause title accordingly.
3. It is also prayed by the appellant substituted today that the
appeal may be allowed to be withdrawn based on the settlement
arrived  at  between  the  parties  with  liberty  to  agitate  the  issue
afresh,  if  at  all  the  settlement  falls  apart.  The  appellant  and
respondent both are represented by Counsel and it is submitted that

--- Page 354 ---
2
the appellant who has been assigned the debt and the respondent
No.1,  have  entered  into  a  consent  agreement  dated  15.07.2026
produced as Annexure A1 along with IA No.200247 of 2026.  The
joint prayer of the appellant and the first respondent, to withdraw
the civil appeal is to facilitate the implementation of the resolution
plan  as  per  the  consent  agreement  in  its  entirety  coupled  with
payment of an additional sum as and by way of additional interest
for the time taken for implementation of the resolution plan.
4. We are not going into the further terms since it has already
been produced as Annexure A1 and is part of the record of these
proceedings. The appeal is allowed to be withdrawn with liberty to
agitate  the  cause  at  the  option  of  either  of  the  parties  on  the
settlement not coming to fruition.
5. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
