IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO(S). 2074-75 of 2026
IN
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(S). 4799-4800 OF 2025
 T. MANJUNATH                            Applicant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA & ANR.             Respondent(s)
       O R D E R
1. Vide judgment dated 10.11.2025, this Court disposed of
the Criminal Appeal No(s). 4799-4800 of 2025, wherein this
Court, inter alia, in paragraphs 39 & 40 (under subheading ‘C’
of ‘Discussion and Analysis’) observed as follows:
“C. Divergent claims regarding the Appointing Authority
39.  Having  held  so,  we  now  proceed  to  advert  to  the
situation that has arisen owing to the inconsistency in the
diverse  claims  regarding  the  authority  who  actually
appointed  the  accused-appellant.  As  noted  hereinabove,
competing  assertions  have  been  advanced  regarding  the
actual appointing authority of the accused-appellant, based
on  the  documents  handed  over  to  the  Court  during  the
course of the hearing of the appeals. While learned counsel
M.A. No(s). 2074-75 of 2026 1

--- Page 362 ---
for  the  State  maintains  that  the  Commissioner  is  the
appointing authority of the accused-appellant, the learned
senior counsel representing the accused appellant, on the
other  hand,  asserts  that  the  appointing  authority  is  the
State Government.
40. In view of this disputed factual scenario, we are of the
considered opinion that, for a proper and effective resolution
of the controversy, it would be expedient in the interest of
justice  to  remit  the  matter  to  the  trial  Court  for  fresh
adjudication  on  the  limited  issue  regarding  the  actual
appointing  authority  of  the  accused-appellant  and  the
consequential bearing thereof on the validity of the sanction
order.”
2. By  way  of  present  miscellaneous  application(s),  the
applicant  seeks  modification  to  the  extent  that  the  words
“appointing” appearing in Subheading ‘C’ of ‘Discussion and
Analysis’, paragraph 39, and paragraph 40 be replaced with
“competent”.  As  also,  the  phrase  “authority  who  actually
appointed the accused-appellant ” in paragraph 39 be replaced
with “authority competent to accord sanction”.
3. In view of the averments made in the application(s), we
deem it appropriate to modify the judgment dated 10.11.2025
to  the  extent  that  the  words  “appointing”  appearing  in
Subheading ‘C’ of ‘Discussion and Analysis’, paragraph 39, and
paragraph 40 be replaced with “ competent” as well as the
phrase  “authority  who  actually  appointed  the  accused-
appellant”  in  paragraph  39  be  replaced  with  “authority
competent to accord sanction”.
4. The remaining contents of the judgment shall remain
unchanged.
M.A. No(s). 2074-75 of 2026 2

--- Page 363 ---
5. The Miscellaneous Application(s) shall stand disposed of
in the above terms.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
....................,J.
       (VIKRAM NATH)
....................,J.
   (SANDEEP MEHTA)
....................,J.
   (R. MAHADEVAN)
  NEW DELHI;
JULY 21, 2026.
M.A. No(s). 2074-75 of 2026 3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal No(s).  3294/2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 12029/2026
FIROZ                                              Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P.                                     Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3.This appeal arises from an order dated
26.02.2026  passed  by  the  High  Court  of
Judicature  at  Allahabad  rejecting  the
regular bail prayer of the appellant in
connection  with  Case  Crime  No.  80/2022,
registered at Dibai, District Bulandshahr.
4. The submission of the learned counsel for
the  appellant  is  that  he  has  suffered
incarceration for a period exceeding 4 years
in a case where minimum sentence is 3 years
1

--- Page 366 ---
and maximum is 10 years. It has also been
submitted that there is no evidence of Cow
Slaughter. The allegation is in respect of
transportation of cattle. 
5. The  learned  Counsel  for  the  State-
respondent has opposed the prayer for bail
by submitting that the appellant has 26
previous criminal antecedents.
6. The  learned  counsel  for  the
appellant, in response, submits that out of
those 26 cases, he has been acquitted in six
cases and has been granted bail in rest of
the cases.
7.  Having  regard  to  the  fact  that  the
appellant has suffered incarceration for a
period exceeding 4 years in this case, we
are  of  the  view  that  the  appellant  is
entitled to be released on bail at this
stage.
2

--- Page 367 ---
8. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The
order passed by the High Court rejecting the
regular bail prayer of the appellant is set
aside.
9.The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court
may deem fit to impose in the facts and
circumstances of the case.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.

of imprisonment, is entitled to be released
on bail at this stage.

2025, titled “Mukhtiyar Urf Mukhiya Vs. State of U.P.”,  passed

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(s).         OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.7904 of 2026 )
  THE STATE OF WEST BENGAL                         APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
  AVIJIT KUNDU ALIAS NITISH & ANR.                 RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the impugned judgment and order dated
29.12.2025 in CRM(NDPS) No.1738 of 2025, titled “ Avijit Kundu @
Nitish & Anr. Vs. The State of West Bengal”, passed by the High
Court at Calcutta.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the parties, we find that the
impugned judgment and order to be absolutely cryptic and non-
speaking, without even referring to the facts; statutory provisions
of the law; and the decision rendered by this Court.
4. As such on this short ground alone, we quash and set aside the
impugned judgment and order dated 29.12.2025 in CRM(NDPS) No.1738
of 2025, titled “Avijit Kundu @ Nitish & Anr. Vs. The State of West
Bengal”,  passed by the High Court at Calcutta,  and remand the
matter to the High Court for consideration afresh, with CRM(NDPS)
No.1738 of 2025 restored to its original number and status.
5. We request the Hon’ble Chief Justice of the High Court at
Calcutta to immediately place the matter before the appropriate
Court for hearing.
6. The parties shall appear before the High Court on 04.08.2026.
1

--- Page 394 ---
7. We further request the High Court to positively decide the
matter within a period of four weeks thereafter.
8. The respondents have been enjoying the liberty of bail since
the passing of the impugned order. They shall continue to do so
till such time, the matter is decided by the High Court, but in any
case not later than 15.09.2026 and shall surrender thereafter if
the matter is not decided. All contentions raised by the parties
are left open.
9. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
.......................J.
(AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 21, 2026.
2

Criminal Appeal No.2741/2026 Page 1 of 3

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Ram Ekbal Roy, learned counsel appearing
for the appellant and Mr. Vikramaditya Chouhan, learned
Additional Advocate General appearing for the respondent-
State.
2. Learned counsel appearing for the appellant submits
that the appeal has already been admitted and that the
appellant has undergone 7 years and 8 months in custody.
The appeal is likely to take some time.  Learned counsel
submits that the case rests on circumstantial evidence.
3. Learned Additional Advocate General appearing for
the respondent-State vehemently opposes the prayer of the
appellant for suspension of sentence and the benefit of
bail.  Learned AAG further submits that discovery in this
case is attributed to the appellant.
4. Having  considered  the  overall  facts  and
circumstances and the period of incarceration and the
fact that the appeal is likely to take some time for
hearing,  we are inclined to suspend the sentence of the
appellant and enlarge him on bail, pending disposal of
the present appeal.
5. Therefore,  the  sentence  of  the  appellant  is
suspended and he is directed to be enlarged on bail,
pending disposal of this appeal.
Criminal Appeal No.2741/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 407 ---
6. The application is disposed of in the above terms.
7. Vide order dated 15 th May, 2026, Trial Court record
has been called for but the same has not been received so
far.
8. Let a reminder be sent to the concerned Trial Court
and after receipt, digital copy of the same be supplied
to the learned counsel for the parties.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  calls  in
question the correctness of the order dated 10th December,
2025 passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna in
Criminal Miscellaneous No.74230 of 2025. By the said
order, the petitioner has been denied the benefit of
regular bail in connection with Barh P.S.Case No.455 of
2025 registered for the offence under Sections 317(5),
336(3) and 340(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
2. Heard  Mr.  Vishal  Arun  Mishra,  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Mr. Anshul Narayan,
learned counsel appearing on advance notice for the
respondent-State.
3. Delay condoned.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the
petitioner has already undergone approximately 1 year in
custody.
5. Learned counsel for the respondent-State submits
that there  are four  criminal antecedents.   We  have
perused the nature of the antecedents. They all are
similar or agnate offences.
SLP (Criminal) D.No.31622/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 410 ---
6. Considering the period undergone and the fact that
the trial is likely to take some time, we are inclined to
enlarge  the  petitioner  on  bail  subject  to  the
satisfaction of the Trial Court, pending trial. 
7. Therefore,  the  petitioner  is  directed  to  be
enlarged on bail subject to the satisfaction of the Trial
Court, pending trial.
8. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
9. Pending application shall also stand disposed of.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  calls  in
question the correctness of the order dated 11 th March
2026  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Madhya  Pradesh,
Principal Seat at Jabalpur in IA No.21346/2025 in CRA
No.10566/2023. By the said order, the prayer of the
petitioner for suspension of sentence and to enlarge the
petitioner on bail has been declined. The petitioner has
been convicted under Sections 8(c) read with Section 27A
read  with  Section  29  of  the  Narcotic  Drugs  and
Psychotropic  Substances  Act,  1985  and  sentenced  to
undergo RI for 10 years and to pay fine of Rs.1,00,000/-
with default stipulation.
2. Delay condoned.
3. Heard  Mr.  Prathvi  Raj  Chauhan,  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner and Mr. Jitendra Kumar
Tripathy, learned counsel appearing on advance notice for
the respondent-State.
4. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  petitioner
submits that the petitioner has already undergone nearly
3 years and 8 months in custody.  Learned counsel further
submits that the petitioner is a lady.  Our attention has
SLP (Criminal) D.38193/2026 Page 2 of 3

--- Page 413 ---
been drawn to the order dated 15 th May, 2026 Annexure-P7
(page  122  of  the  paper  book)  where  the  co-accused
Dharmendra  was  given  the  benefit  of  suspension  of
sentence.
5. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State
vehemently opposes the prayer for suspension of sentence.
Learned counsel submits that the co-accused Dharmendra
has undergone more than half of the sentence imposed.
6. Having  considered  the  overall  facts  and
circumstances, we are inclined to suspend the sentence of
the petitioner and enlarge her on bail, pending hearing
of the Criminal Appeal No.10566 of 2023 before the High
Court.
7. The sentence imposed on the petitioner is suspended
and the petitioner is directed to be enlarged on bail
subject to the satisfaction of the Trial Court, pending
disposal of the Criminal Appeal by the High Court.
8. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
9. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.

T P(Civil)  No.3147/2024
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. By our order dated 13.05.2026, we had made ourselves
abundantly clear that the amount of Rs.15 lakh offered by the
husband is not reasonable.  We said so keeping in mind that
there is a minor daughter that the petitioner-mother has to
take care of.  The petitioner left her matrimonial home way
back in 2023. She has no other means of any income.  In such
circumstances, today we have made ourselves very clear to Mr.
Nishant Soni, who has joined the proceedings online that he has
to arrange for Rs.25,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty five lakhs only)
towards full and final settlement.
2. We  give  one  last  opportunity  to  him  to  raise  the
necessary finance and make this payment to his wife and minor
daughter. 
3. Today, in the course of hearing, the learned counsel
appearing for the petitioner brought something very disturbing
to our notice. It appears that the petitioner has got herself
entangled in Section 138 NI Act complaints.  
4. We take notice of the fact that Criminal Case Nos.
5662/2025, 1387/2026 and 1097/2026 respectively are pending  in
the Court of 46-3rd Additional Senior Civil Judge and Additional
Chief Judicial Magistrate, Bhuj, Kutch/1-Principal Senior Civil
2

--- Page 419 ---
T P(Civil)  No.3147/2024
Judge, Civil Court, Bhuj, Kutch/49 th-2nd Additional Civil Judge
and Chief Judicial Magistrate First Class, Bhuj, Kutch.  There
are in all three complaints lodged against her. The allegations
are  that  the  respondent  husband  is  responsible  for  this
proceedings against his own wife through one individual by name
Solanki Tulsi Rajesh.
5. We stay further proceedings of all the three criminal
cases including the non- bailable warrants issued by the courts
below.
6. On the next date of hearing, Solanki Tulsi Rajesh, the
original complainant of the three NI Act complaints, shall
appear before this Court through Video Conferencing.
7. Registry shall forward one copy of this order to the
Superintendent of Police, Bhuj, Kutch, who in turn shall inform
Solanki Tulsi Rajesh  of the order passed by this Court today
and further that she has to appear before this Court on
17.08.2026  through  Video Conferencing. 
8. List the matter on 17.08.2026.
(CHANDRESH)               (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

1. Delay condoned.
2. The present petition has been filed for grant of
bail.
3. The petitioner has been in jail for almost one
and  a  half  year  in  connection  with  NCB  Crime  No.
05/NCB/Kol/2025  dated  26.03.2025,  registered  for  the
offence punishable under Section 8(c) read with Sections
20(b)(ii)(c)/29 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic
Substances Act, 1985.
4. A recovery of 29 kg of ganja was effected from
the petitioner’s house.
5. Learned  Additional  Solicitor  General  appearing
for the respondent-State submits that the petitioner has
three criminal antecedents, one of which has not been
disclosed by the petitioner.
6. After hearing learned counsel for the parties,
this Court is of the opinion that the application for
grant of bail deserves to be allowed and is accordingly
allowed.
7. Accordingly, we direct that the petitioner be
enlarged on bail in connection with aforesaid crime , on
such terms and conditions as may be imposed by the Trial
Court, subject to the condition that the petitioner
2

--- Page 422 ---
shall  continue  to  co-operate  with  the
trial/investigation.
8. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

         UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Delay condoned.
SLP(C) Diary No. 31363/2024 had come up before this Court on
1

--- Page 427 ---
M.A. Diary No. 49106/2025
03.02.2025 and on the said date, the learned counsel for the
parties were directed to ascertain as to whether the adjustment of
2.95% was given during the period after 2016. The learned counsel
for the petitioners were also asked to ascertain as to whether the
respondents regularly paid the tax at the rate of 4% and thereupon
sought benefit of tax at the rate of 01.05% under the Scheme.
The matter then came up before this Court on 14.02.2025.  It
is  an  admitted  position  that  the  State  of  Haryana  and  its
authorities, the petitioners, did not file an affidavit as on that
date pursuant to the order dated 03.02.2025 and only one affidavit
filed by Suncity Buildcon Pvt. Ltd. was before this Court.  The
other assessees, viz., Essel Project Pvt. Ltd., Suncity Projects
Pvt. Ltd. and ABW Suncity, did not file separate affidavits as to
the factual position obtaining in their cases.  However, under the
impression that the affidavit filed by Suncity Buildcon Pvt. Ltd.
covered the issue entirely, the special leave petitions were
dismissed.
The present miscellaneous applications have been filed by the
State  of  Haryana  and  its  authorities  seeking  recall  of  the
aforestated order dated 14.02.2025 in these circumstances. 
We  are  of  the  opinion  that  the  applications  merit
consideration as the full facts were not placed before this Court
as on the date the order dated 14.02.2025 was passed.  The said
order is, accordingly, recalled and the special leave petitions are
restored to their original number for consideration afresh.
The miscellaneous applications shall stand disposed of.
2

--- Page 428 ---
M.A. Diary No. 49106/2025
As the special leave petitions stand restored, issue notice to
the respondents, returnable on 05.10.2026.
Notice shall be served by all modes, including dasti.
In the meanwhile, pleadings shall also be completed.
(DEEPAK GUGLANI)                             (PREETI SAXENA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                         COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
[Arising out of SLP(CRL.) No.6128/2026]
PIYUSH KUMAR VERMA APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS
THE STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant is aggrieved by the judgment and order
dated 31.10.2025 passed by a Division Bench of the High
Court of Judicature at Allahabad, Lucknow, in terms
whereof, Criminal Writ Petition filed by the appellant
has been summarily disposed of on the ground that he had
earlier approached the High Court on three occasions for
quashing of the History Sheet No.376A/2009 at Police
Station Jankipuram, Lucknow and appropriate orders were
passed in those writ petitions. The High Court has
reproduced the previous order passed in one of the cases
and  has  accordingly  dismissed  the  appellant’s  writ
petition without going into the question whether the
history  sheet  under  challenge  is  liable  to  be
quashed/closed  in  light  of  the  supervening
circumstances, including acquittal of the appellant vide
judgment dated 05.07.2014 in Sessions Case No.584A/2008
arising from FIR No.365/2007.
3. Though in the counter affidavit, the respondent-
State has taken the plea that active surveillance and
routine  monitoring  against  the  appellant  has  been
formally closed and discontinued on 17.10.2021, however,
it is insisted that the history sheet is a permanent
informational  repository  and,  thus,  it  cannot  be
1

--- Page 433 ---
destroyed.  This  issue,  in  our  considered  opinion,
deserves to be adjudicated by the High Court on the
merits after considering the rival contentions.
4. Consequently, the appeal is allowed. The impugned
judgment and order of the High Court is set aside, and
the matter is remitted to the High Court for fresh
adjudication of the Criminal Miscellaneous Writ Petition
No.1356/2024. We request the High Court to decide the
petition expeditiously and preferably within six months.
 
..........................CJI.
(SURYA KANT)
............................J.
(JOYMALYA BAGCHI) 
............................J.
(V. MOHANA) 
       
NEW DELHI;        
JULY 20, 2026
2

--- Page 434 ---

1. Learned  Senior  Counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner(s) at the outset has stated before this Court
that the petitioner(s) shall certainly vacate the premises
in question, however, by way of last indulgence, extension
upto 31 st of December, 2027 be granted in the matter,
subject to payment of enhanced rent i.e. Rs. 1,70,000/- per
month.
2. Learned counsel for the respondent(s) has vehemently
opposed the aforesaid prayer. She has stated that earlier
also extensions have been granted in the matter.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner(s) has prayed for
grant of extension upto 31.12.2027, however, keeping in
view the totality of circumstances of the case and to bring
the lis to an end, one last extension is granted upto
31.12.2026 subject to payment of Rs. 1,70,000/- per month.
4. The petitioner(s) shall file an undertaking before
the Registry of this Court positively within two weeks from
today, to the said effect.
5. As in the present case, large number of extensions
have been granted from time to time, this court is making
it clear that no further extension of any kind will be
granted in future.
6. It is further made clear that if the undertaking
filed by the petitioner(s) is violated, this Court shall be
taking suo moto cognizance for initiating contempt of court
2

--- Page 446 ---
proceedings against the petitioner(s).

NANDIPATI SHAMILI                                    Petitioner(s)

NANDIPATI SIVA KUMAR                                 Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. The present petition has been filed for transfer of case
bearing Petition A/750/2024, titled as “Nandipati Siva Kumar
vs. Nandipati Shamili” , pending before the Family Court,
Bandra, Mumbai to the court of Senior Civil Judge at Nuzvid,
Andhra Pradesh. 
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that there are
three other cases pending at Nuzvid, Andhra Pradesh and the
present petition filed by the husband for grant of divorce is
pending before the Family Court, Bandra, Mumbai. 
3. Learned counsel for the respondent vehemently opposed the
prayer for transfer. 
4. In the considered opinion of this court, the present
transfer petition deserves to be allowed.
5. Accordingly, we allow the transfer petition and direct
the transfer of case bearing Petition A/750/2024, titled as
“Nandipati Siva Kumar vs. Nandipati Shamili” , pending before
the Family Court, Bandra, Mumbai to the court of Senior Civil
Judge at Nuzvid, Andhra Pradesh. 

--- Page 448 ---
6. Let the record of the case be transferred without delay.
7. A copy of this order be sent to the concerned court for
compliance. If video conferencing facility is available with
the transferee Court, the benefit of the same shall be
extended to the parties on such dates when their physical
appearance is not required. It is made clear that this Court
has not expressed any opinion on the merits of the matter.
8. The parties shall also be free to explore possibility of
mediation in the matter.
9. As three cases are pending at Nuzvid, Andhra Pradesh and
the divorce case is also being transferred to the same place,
the learned District Judge/concerned Court shall grant the
same date in all four cases pending between the parties.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

ITEM NO.49               COURT NO.11               SECTION II-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  8437/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  21-03-2025
in CRM No. 87634/2024 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Patna]
AMAN KUMAR                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR                                 Respondent(s)
IA No. 138015/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
Mr. Sidharth Luthra, Sr. Adv. (Amicus Curiae)
               Mr. S Nagamuthu, Sr. Adv. (Amicus Curiae) 
               Mr. D L Chidananda, AOR
               Mr. Mohd, Ashab, Adv.
               Ms. Mrinalika Dwivedi, Adv.
               Mr. Rajat Mathur, Adv.
               Mr. Pranshu Dwivedi, Adv.
               Mr. Raghav Sharma, Adv.
               Mr. Bilal Mansoor, Adv.
               Mr. Shreyas Kaushal, Adv.
               Mr. Ishaan Roy Choudhary, Adv.             
              
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Somesh Chandra Jha, AOR
                   Mr. Akash Kishore, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhishek Kr Srivastava, Adv.
                   Mr. Uttam Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Tatsat Shukla, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Azmat Hayat Amanullah, AOR
                   Ms. Rebecca Mishra, Adv.
                                      
                   Mr. Arvind Kumar Sharma, AOR
                   Mr. Tushar Mehta, Solicitor General
                   Mr. Bhuvan Kapoor, Adv.
                   Ms. Nasadiya Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Medha Pushkarna, Adv.
                   Ms. Shilpi Ohri, Adv.
                   Mr. Chitvan Singhal, Adv.
1

--- Page 460 ---
                   Ms. Ameyavikrama Thanvi, Adv.
                   Mr. Raman Yadav, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhishek Kumar Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Kartikay Aggarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Dr. N. Visakamurthy-aor, Adv.               
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1.  This  Court  having  noticed  that  in  the  instant  case  the
chargesheet came to be filed on 13.09.2024, yet the charge had not
been framed till 29.10.2025 and after taking note that even after
months and years, after filing of the chargesheet, the charges are
not being framed, which is the basis on which the trail get delayed
and such scenario is prevalent in many of the courts across the
country, was of the view that directions requires to be issued PAN
India and as such had requested Mr. Sidharth Luthra and Mr. S
Nagamuthu, learned senior counsels to assist the court as amicus
curiae and painstakingly they have prepared the reports after
compiling  the  statistics  collected  from  all  High  Courts  and
collated them with an able assistance from their team which was an
arduous task. In fact this Court had also directed the Registrar
Generals of the High Courts to furnish the information and details,
as may be sought for by the learned amicus curiae and/or their
team.
2. In this regard, we had requested the Hon’ble Chief Justices of
all the High Courts to constitute a Committee for this purpose and
to forward the information and on based on such inputs received
they have prepared two reports dated 19.05.2026 and 29.05.2026,
which has been placed on record. Perusal of the said reports would
2

--- Page 461 ---
indicate the broad outlines of directions to be issued to the
Courts, have been enumerated – stage wise.
3. Mr. Nagamuthu, learned senior counsel has also brought to our
notice the importance of the role of lawyers in the administration
of justice and the role that may be played by the learned
advocates, if and when the directions are issued by this court
would be of vital importance. The direction that may be issued
after hearing all the concerned, would result in the respective
High  Courts  implementing  the  same  and  as  such  the
administrative/portfolio High Court Judges will have a very key
role to play in the implementation of the directions that would be
issued.
4. In that view of the matter, we issue notice to the Bar Council
of India and all the learned Registrar Generals of the High Courts
in this petition to enable them to put forth their views and to
make their submissions on the aforesaid two reports namely report
dated 19.05.2026 and 29.05.2026, the copies of which shall be
furnished  alongwith  the  notice  that  would  be  issued  by  the
Registry.
5. List on 25.08.2026.
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 462 ---

      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Heard  Mr. Naresh Kumar Tomar,  learned counsel for
the  petitioner  and  Mr.  Kartikeya  Rastogi, learned
counsel for the State.
3. This petition calls in question the correctness of

--- Page 472 ---
2
the order dated  27-11-2025 in CRMP No. 3189/2025 in
Crl.Appeal No.80/2024 passed by the High Court of
Himachal Pradesh at Shimla. By the said order, the
prayer for suspension of sentence and enlargement of
bail of the petitioner was rejected.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner states that the
petitioner has undergone about six years and four
months out of the sentence imposed upon him of ten
years RI. Learned counsel submits that it is a fixed
sentence. The appeal is likely to take some time,
hence, the suspension of sentence may be considered,
taking into account the period already undergone by the
petitioner.
5. Learned counsel for the State vehemently defence the
impugned order.
6. Having considered the facts and circumstances of the
case, we are inclined to suspend the sentence and
enlarge the petitioner on bail pending disposal of
Criminal Appeal No.80/2024 before the High Court.
7.  Accordingly,  we  direct  that  the  sentence  of
petitioner is suspended and petitioner be enlarged on
bail pending disposal of the Criminal Appeal No.80/2024
before the High Court subject to the satisfaction of
the  concerned  Trial  Court  in  ND&PS  Trial
No.80/2021/2020 (CIS NDPS No.38/2020)  for the offences
punishable under Sections 20(b)(ii)(C) of the Narcotic
Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985.

--- Page 473 ---
3
8. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          /2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.4903/2026]
VISHNU AGRAWAL                                APPELLANT(S)
                          VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                         RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 13.02.2026
passed by the High Court of Chhattisgarh at Bilaspur
rejecting the anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in
connection with Case Crime No.14/2026 registered at Police
Station – Chhuriya, District Rajnandgaon (C.G.).
4. After  considering  the  submission  of  the  learned
counsel for the appellant, on 23.03.2026, this Court had
passed the following order:
“1. Issue notice, returnable in six
weeks. 
2. In the meantime, it is provided
that in the event the petitioner is
arrested  in  connection  with  Crime
No.14/2024  registered  at  Police
Station  Chhuriya,  District
Rajnandgaon, Chhattisgarh, he shall
be released on a personal bond of Rs.
25,000/- subject to an undertaking
that  he  shall  cooperate  in  the
investigation  and  make  himself
available for interrogation as and
when required by the investigating
agency.  He  shall  also  submit  an

--- Page 487 ---
undertaking that he will not threaten
the  witnesses  or  tamper  the
evidence.”
5. On service of notice, the State is represented.  We
have considered his submission.  
6. Upon consideration of submissions, we do not find a
good  reason  to  discharge  the  interim  protection  order
granted earlier.
7.  This appeal is, accordingly, disposed of by making
the interim protection order dated 23.03.2026 absolute
subject to the following conditions:
(A) Within three weeks from today, the appellant shall
submit bail bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial
Court along with an undertaking that he shall co-
operate in the trial/investigation; and 
(B) He shall submit an additional undertaking that he will
not threaten the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
……………………………………………………………………………J
                            [MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………………………………J
                                     [VIJAY BISHNOI]
New Delhi
JULY 22, 2026

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.6627/2012
M/S CGGC SOMA JV  APPELLANT
                                VERSUS
COMMERCIAL TAX OFFICER & ORS. RESPONDENTS
WITH
CIVIL APPEAL NOS.6628-6631/2012
CIVIL APPEAL NO.6632/2012
O R D E R
1. Having perused the impugned judgment dated 27.06.2011, we
notice  that  following  earlier  judgment  rendered  in  W.P.
No.12455/2005  (batch  matters),  the  judgment  impugned  in  the
present appeal, i.e., W.P. No.18479/2007 came to be disposed of.
The said judgment rendered in W.P. No.12455/2005 and connected
matters including W.P. No.16888/2011 came to be adjudicated by
this Court in C.A. Nos.8267-8271/2012 and connected matters. This
Court vide judgment dated 07.02.2018, following the Nine Judges’
Bench Judgment of this Court remitted the matters to the High
Court by observing thus:
“Having perused the impugned judgment dated 22.06.2011,
we are of the view that after the nine-Judge Bench
judgment of this Court, wherein the compensatory nature of
Entry Tax was expressly overruled, it would be difficult
for us to hear these appeals without fresh material being
placed. A co-ordinate Bench of this Court, by an Order
dated  29.03.2017,  has  in  similar  cases,  stated  that
appropriate course of action in such cases would be to
permit the appellants to file fresh petitions raising
other  issues  that  arise,  with  the  necessary  factual
background or any other constitutional/statutory issue
which arises for consideration. We may point out that the
1

--- Page 490 ---
impugned judgment itself has remanded the matters to
determine  the  nature  of  the  particular  earth  moving
vehicles and whether they could be said to be “motor
vehicle”  under  the  Motor  Vehicles  Act.  We  are  also
informed that I.As have been filed in this Court raising
other pleas. This Court has extended the interim order,
which was already obtained, and we permit the persons who
have filed those I.As to go by way of writ petitions to
the High Court within eight weeks from today. Interim
order to continue till then.
The civil appeals are, accordingly, disposed of.” 
2. A  perusal  of  the  aforesaid  order  would  indicate  that
appropriate course of action would be to permit the appellant(s)
to file fresh petition raising other issues that arise, with
necessary  factual  background  or  any  other  constitutional  /
statutory  issue which arises for consideration reiterating the
order of remand made by the High Court. Hence, we dispose of these
appeals as ordered in Civil Appeal Nos.8267-8271/2012 vide order
dated 07.02.2018. This Court has extended the interim order which
was already obtained and the said interim orders to continue for a
period of eight weeks from today.
3. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
.................J.
  (ARAVIND KUMAR)
.................J.
(VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026.

O R D E R
1. This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908. The petitioner has filed this Transfer
Petition seeking the following reliefs:
“Transfer  the  case  bearing  C.P.  No.  183/2026,
titled as “Subhankar Mishra v. Divya Tripathi”,
pending before the Family Judge-II, Bhubaneswar at
Khudra,  Odisha  to  the  Principal  Judge,  Family
Court, Gorakhpur, U.P.”
2. Having heard the learned counsel for the petitioner and on
perusal of the material on record, we find that the petitioner has
made out a case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
3. In the result, the Transfer Petition is allowed and C.P.
No. 183/2026, titled as “Subhankar Mishra v. Divya Tripathi”,
pending before the Family Judge-II, Bhubaneswar at Khudra, Odisha
is  hereby  transferred  to  the  Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,
1

--- Page 499 ---
Gorakhpur, U.P.
4. Registry to communicate this order to the transferor court
for taking steps in accordance with this order.
5. On an application made by the respondent, the Court to
which the case is transferred shall permit the respondent to appear
through video-conference on all dates except on the dates on which
his personal presence is absolutely mandatory.
6. The transfer petition stands allowed in the aforesaid
terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

                   Mr. Porus, Adv.
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. This  Court  by  order  dated  09.07.2026  had
required the petitioner to deposit the cheque amount of
Rs.6,00,000/- (Rupees Six Lakhs Only) within a period
of four weeks and further to file an undertaking within
a week to that effect.
2. Today,  learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner
submits  that  Rs.1,40,000/-  (Rupees  One  Lakh  Forty
1

--- Page 508 ---
 
Thousand Only) was deposited upon an appeal being filed
before the Sessions Court on 31.05.2025. The said
amount of Rs.1,40,000/- (Rupees One Lakh Forty Thousand
Only) has since been released to the complainant. He,
therefore, submits today that the petitioner may be
allowed to deposit an amount of Rs.4,60,000/- (Rupees
Four Lakhs Sixty Thousand Only), being the balance
amount of Rs.6,00,000/- (Rupees Six Lakhs Only) as
Rs.1,40,000/- (Rupees One Lakh Forty Thousand Only) has
already been deposited and paid to the petitioner.
3. We have noticed from the impugned order that the
revision petition no.689 of 2026 before the High Court
was dismissed solely on the ground that the petitioner
failed to deposit 50% of the amount directed by the
High Court while entertaining the revision petition and
suspending the sentence.
4. Since the petitioner is ready and willing to
deposit the balance cheque amount of Rs.4,60,000/-
(Rupees Four Lakhs Sixty Thousand Only) within three
weeks  from  today,  we  dispose  of  this  petition
permitting the petitioner to deposit the said amount
before the High Court on or before 13.08.2026. In case,
the  said  amount  is  deposited  and  an  appropriate
application is filed by the petitioner along with copy
of this order, the High Court shall restore the said
revision petition and hear and decide the same on
2

--- Page 509 ---
 
merits. The amount so deposited shall be paid to the
complainant within four weeks of its being deposited
before the High Court.
5. In case the amount is not deposited before the
13.08.2026, the sentence awarded by the Trial Court
shall be executed forthwith and the High Court shall
not entertain the revision petition any longer.
6. The  special  leave  petition  and  pending
application(s) stand disposed of.
(NEETU KHAJURIA)

      (RANJANA SHAILEY)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition has been
filed seeking grant of anticipatory bail to the
petitioner in connection with FIR No.483/2025 dated
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.)  NO.6835/2026

--- Page 517 ---
2
13.05.2025, registered at Police Station Danapur,
District Patna, Bihar, for the offences punishable
under Sections 109, 126(2), 118(1), 118(2), and
303(2), read with Section 3(5) of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.  By order dated 27.04.2026,
this Court directed that no coercive steps shall be
taken against the petitioner. The petitioner was
also  directed  to  join  the  investigation  and
cooperate with the Investigating Officer.
2. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent–State of Bihar fairly submits that the
petitioner has joined the investigation and has
appeared before the Investigating Officer ("I.O.")
as and when called upon. 
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits
that it was not the petitioner who assaulted the
deceased and that there is no evidence against him.
On  the  other  hand,  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent–State of Bihar has vehemently opposed
the application for grant of anticipatory bail.
4. Having heard the learned counsel for the
parties at length and taking into account the fact
that the petitioner has been cooperating with the
investigation, we deem it appropriate to make the
interim order dated 27.04.2026 absolute.
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.)  NO.6835/2026

--- Page 518 ---
3
5. In the event of the petitioner's arrest
pursuant to the aforesaid FIR, we direct that he
shall be released on bail, subject to such terms
and conditions as may be imposed by the concerned
Trial Court.
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in
the above terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also
stand disposed of.
(JAGDISH KUMAR)                                 (KOMAL)

     UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present Special Leave Petition has been
filed seeking the grant of bail to the petitioner
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.)  NO.6920/2026

--- Page 520 ---
2
in  connection  with  FIR  No.27/2025  dated
13.07.2025, registered at Police Station Chilphi,
District Kabirdham, Chhattisgarh, for the offence
punishable  under  Section  20(B)(ii)(c)  of  the
Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act,
1985.  The  petitioner  has  been  in  jail  since
15.07.2025.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits
before  this  Court  that  there  is  no  evidence
against the petitioner except the statement of the
co-accused.  He  further  submits  that  all  the
seizure witnesses have turned hostile. It is also
contended that the trial is likely to take a long
time to conclude and that the petitioner is an
innocent person.
3. Ms. Ankita Sharma, learned counsel appearing
for the respondent–State of Chhattisgarh, fairly
submits that the present petitioner was made an
accused solely on the basis of the statement of
the  co-accused.  She  has  not  disputed  the
submissions  made  by  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner.
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.)  NO.6920/2026

--- Page 521 ---
3
4. In  light  of  the  aforesaid  facts  and
circumstances, the application for grant of bail
deserves  to  be  allowed  and  is,  accordingly,
allowed. The petitioner is directed to be released
on bail forthwith, subject to the usual terms and
conditions to be imposed by the concerned Trial
Court.
5. The  present  Special  Leave  Petition  is,
accordingly, disposed of, along with all pending
application(s), if any.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO.   1494/2025  
SHRUTI SACHIN SHARMA                        PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
SACHIN DEVKINANDAN SHARMA                  RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This  transfer  petition  has  been  filed  by  the
petitioner-wife seeking transfer of Petition A 792 of
2024 titled “Sachin Devkinandan Sharma vs. Shruti Sachin
Sharma” from the Principal Judge, Family Court, Nashik
Court, Maharashtra to the Family Court, Indore, Madhya
Pradesh.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties and perused
the material placed on record.
3. We have considered the facts of the case and grounds
on which transfer has been sought. On such consideration,
we are satisfied with the grounds urged by the petitioner
and accept the same. Therefore, the case bearing Petition
A 792 of 2024  titled  “Sachin Devkinandan Sharma vs.
Shruti Sachin Sharma”  pending before the Family Court,
Nashik Court, Maharashtra, is ordered to be transferred
to the Family Court, Indore, Madhya Pradesh.
4. Records  of  the  case  should  immediately  be
transferred to the transferee court.
1

--- Page 526 ---
5. The  parties  may  explore  the  possibility  of
settlement through Mediation. The transferee court would
obtain the desire and wishes of the parties and proceed
accordingly in this regard.
6. The  parties  are  at  liberty  initially  to  appear
through virtual mode. If such request is made, the
concerned court may favourably consider the same and
thereafter, if need be, the parties can be asked to
appear through physical mode.
7. The Transfer Petition is allowed accordingly.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
....................,J.
       (VIKRAM NATH)
....................,J.
   (SANDEEP MEHTA)
  NEW DELHI;
JULY 23, 2026.

RSA No. 3439 of 1998
Counter affidavit was filed by respondent nos. 1 and 2 after
the opportunity was already declined. The same be apprised before
the Hon’ble Court.
Mr. P. N. Puri, learned Advocate has filed the counter
affidavit on behalf of all the proposed Lrs. of deceased respondent
no.9, but has filed vakalatnama only for proposed Lr. nos. 9(i) to
9(iii). Service on proposed Lr. nos. 9(iv) to 9(vi) is complete,
but none has entered appearance.

--- Page 532 ---
2
In the last order of this Registrar’s Court, learned counsel
for the petitioner was directed to take appropriate steps in
respect of proposed Lr. no. 9(vii) under Order LIII, Sub-Rule 3,
Second Proviso of the Supreme Court Rules. But, as per the office
report, the same steps were not taken.
However, it is seen that notice on proposed Lr. no. 9(vii) was
served through usual mode. Therefore, service is deemed complete on
proposed Lr. no. 9(vii).

and proposed Lr. nos. 16(i to iii), but no one has entered
appearance.

Hon’ble Judge-in-Chamber’s order dated 15.03.2024.
RSA No. 3434 of 1998

ITEM NO.105               COURT NO.12               SECTION XII-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Civil Appeal  No(s).  10110-10111/2011
M/S NATIONAL TRADERS                               Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA                             Respondent(s)
 
Date : 16-07-2026 These appeals were called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARAVIND KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Appellant(s) : Mr. Yashraj Singh Deora, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Anupama Dhurve, Adv.
                   Mr. Sriram Krishna, Adv.
                   Mr. Priyesh Mohan Srivastava, Adv.
                   M/s Mitter & Mitter Co., AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Prateek Chadha, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Rajesh Gulab Inamdar, AOR
                   Mr. Sreekar Aechuri, Adv.
                   Mr. Aniket Chauhaan, Adv.
                   Ms. Surbhi Soni, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashwat Anand, Adv.
                   Ms. Sheena Taqui, Adv.
                   Ms. Akansha Saini, Adv.
                   Mr. B L N Sanjit, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The issue in the present appeal relates to levy of sales tax on
the sale of old newspapers under the Karnataka Sales Tax Act, 1957
and Central Sales Tax Act, 1956 relating to the assessment year
2000-2001 to 2002-2003.
1

--- Page 562 ---
2. The appellant claimed exemption based on the judgment of this
Court in  Sait Rikhaji Furtarnal and another  v.  State of Andhra
Pradesh, (1992) 85 STC 1:(1991) Suppl. (1) SCC 202. The authorities
rejected the exemption claimed and levied tax relying on the
judgment of this Court rendered in the case of  Indian Express
Private Limited v. State of Tamil Nadu , (1987) 67 STC 474:(1986)
SCC Online SC 3 and also the order of this Court in the case of The
Hindu v. State of Tamil Nadu, (1987) SCC Online SC 13:(1987) 67 STC
477, which virtually followed the judgment rendered in  Indian
Express (supra).
3. However, in the instant case, the jurisdictional High Court has
reversed the order of the tribunal and allowed the State’s revision
petition by referring to the judgment of this Court in  Indian
Express (supra) and holding that sale of old newspaper is exigible
to sales tax which has led to filing of the present appeal.
4. Though, the learned counsel appearing for the State would submit
that perusal of the two judgments and applying the principles laid
thereunder to the facts on hand, it can be reconciled, we are of
the considered view that the judgment rendered in  Sait Rikhaji
(supra) omitted to take note of the law laid down in Indian Express
(supra) and thus divergent views have emerged, though it seems to
be on two different facets, yet the conclusion that has been
arrived at, seems to be relating to sale of ‘old newspaper’, which
was the subject matter of scrutiny by the jurisdictional High Court
in the instant case.
2

--- Page 563 ---
5. Hence, for an authoritative pronouncement of law on this issue,
the matter requires to be examined by a three-Judge Bench inasmuch
as both the judgments referred to hereinabove, namely, Sait Rikhaji
and Indian Express, had been rendered by two Hon’ble Judges of this
Court.
6. Hence, the Registry is directed to place this matter before
Hon’ble the Chief Justice of India for constituting appropriate
Bench or listing this matter before a three-Judge Bench so as to
give quietus to the issue referred to hereinabove.
7. On receipt of the opinion that may be rendered by the larger
Bench, the matter may be placed before this Court for passing
further orders.
(RASHI GUPTA)                                   (AVGV RAMU)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 564 ---
ITEM NO.1707               COURT NO.12               SECTION III
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Civil Appeal  No(s).6720/2023
SHANKAR SINGH SENGAR & ORS.                        APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
PRAMOD AGRAWAL & ORS.                              RESPONDENT(S)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NOS.                OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (C) Nos.18042-18043 of 2026)
DIVYESH JAYKANT SHETH & ANR.                  APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
VEENA HARKISHIN BHATIA                        RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The  appellants  challenge  the  judgment  and  order  dated
24.04.2026 in Civil Revision Application No. 523/2022 with Interim
Application No. 19993 of 2022 titled “Divyesh Jaykant Sheth & Anr.
vs. Veena Harkishin Bhatia” passed by the High Court of Judicature
at Bombay. 
3. We have heard learned counsel for the appellants.
4. Finding the Bench not to be in agreement with the appellants,
the appellants, through learned counsel, seeks time to hand over
the vacant and peaceful possession of the demised premises on or
before 15th July, 2027 to the respondent/landlord. Prayer accepted.
5. The appellants through learned counsel, shall furnish an
undertaking before this Court to vacate the premises on or before
15th July, 2027. The undertaking, in usual form, shall be filed by
the appellants within three weeks from today, failing which the
Registry shall take appropriate steps.
1

--- Page 571 ---
6. The appellants, through learned counsel present in the Court,
have been made aware of the consequences of breach of such an
undertaking, including initiation of contempt proceedings.
7. The arrears of rent due and payable, if any, be positively
cleared within three weeks from today.
8. Needless to add, till such time the appellants occupy the
premises, they shall continue to pay occupational charges at the
rate at which the rent was agreed to be paid.
9. The appeals are disposed of accordingly.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
............................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)         
............................J.
(NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH) 
NEW DELHI;
JULY 16, 2026

DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIPUL M. PANCHOLI
For Petitioner(s) :Petitioner-in-person
                    
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Vikramjit Banerji, A.S.G.

                   Dr. N. Visakamurthy, AOR
                   Mr. Vikramjeet Banerjee, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Rajan Kumar Chourasia, Adv.
1

--- Page 583 ---
                   Mr. Bhakti Vardhan Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Shubhendu Ananad, Adv.
                   Mr. Sudarshan Lamba(aor), Adv.
                                      
                   Mr. Vikramjit Bannerjee, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Sudarshan Lamba, AOR
                   Mr. Rajan Kumar Choursia, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashok Kumar B, Adv.
                   Mr. Shashank Bajpai, Adv.
                   Mr. P V Yogeshwaran, Adv.
                   Mr. Amit Sharma B, Adv.
                   Mr. Shubhendu Ananad, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhakti Vardhan Singh, Adv.
                   
                   Mr. Raj Bahadur Yadav, AOR                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. We, at the outset, point out that the instant writ petition
has been filed  bona fidely  in larger public interest. Mr Gyan
Prakash – the petitioner appears to be a well-known social activist
and a fighter for the downtrodden, who firmly believes in good
governance. He earlier filed a writ petition before the Madhya
Pradesh High Court and pursuant to his initiative, a Division Bench
of the High Court vide judgment dated 05.10.2006 directed the
creation of the subject fund. That judgment is reported as Gyan
Prakash vs. General Mananger, Ordnance Factory, Khamaria & Ors.  -
Indian Law Reports (2006) Madhya Pradesh 1485.
2. The controversy in this case pertains to the utilisation/non-
utilisation of Environmental Relief Fund, which appears to have
been levied and collected under the provisions of the Public
Liability Insurance Act, 1991. 
3. We are informed by counsel for the Union of India that the
total amount collected up to the year 2021 was approximately
Rs.1000.69 crores. He, however, is not aware of the latest figure.
2

--- Page 584 ---
Though a scheme known as “The Environmental Relief Fund Scheme,
2008” was formulated, it appears that no amount thereunder has been
reimburse/released. The aforesaid fund is stated to be entrusted to
Central Pollution Control Board. There is nothing on record to
suggest how much of the amount out of it has been disbursed or
utilised for the purpose for which the fund has been created. 
4. We,  therefore,  direct  the  Union  of  India  to  file  a
comprehensive  affidavit  explaining  the  scheme,  if  any,  for
utilisation of the aforesaid fund. 
5. We also direct the Member Secretary, Central Pollution Control
Board, as well as the Union of India, to assist us with the details
with respect to the utilisation of the aforesaid amount as per the
scheme. 
6. Ms. Aparna Bhat, learned Senior Counsel, is requested to
assist us as an Amicus Curiae in the matter.
7. Post the matter on 29.09.2026.
(ARJUN BISHT)                              (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR
3

--- Page 585 ---

the respondent directly, in writing, apprising her about the
pendency of the present petition; passing of this order; her
right of engaging a counsel of choice; and her right of being
represented, subject to eligibility, through a Legal Aid Counsel.
4. Learned  amicus curiae  shall also get in touch with the
Chairman of the concerned District Legal Services Authority, who
shall have the above said respondent apprised of her rights to
the aforesaid effect.
5. We clarify that the learned amicus curiae shall continue to
assist the Court, even if the respondent is represented by a
legally engaged counsel.
6. The Registry shall communicate this order to all concerned
forthwith.
7. The Registry shall print the name of the learned  amicus
curiae in the cause list.

learned amicus curiae.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No(s). 24540/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  31-10-2025
in WP(C) No. 16534/2025 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New 
Delhi]
DINESH CHANDRA                                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STAFF SELECTION COMMISSION & ANR.                  Respondent(s)
IA No. 132408/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING, IA No.
132409/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED JUDGMENT
 
Date : 21-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. T.N.Tripathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Dushyant Swaroop, Adv.
                   Mr. Saurabh Tripathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Rameshwar Prasad Goyal, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Heard   Mr.  T.N.Tripathi,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner. Learned counsel submits that it is an undisputed
case where the petitioner was selected and along with the
names of other selectees, the name of the petitioner was sent
for police verification. While the names of others came back
after police verification and they have been duly appointed,
the name of the petitioner never came back from the police
verification  and  on  that  ground  he  has  been  denied

--- Page 628 ---
appointment. Learned counsel submits that no follow-up was
done by the department concerned. learned counsel submits that
the Central Administrative Tribunal, Principal Bench, New
Delhi (for short, ‘Tribunal’) rightly ordered the employment
of the petitioner. Learned counsel further submits that even
the High Court has recorded in para 22 of the impugned order
that the petitioner has been unfairly denied the empanelment.
Having so recorded, the High Court has only directed that
whenever  the  respondent  will  need  to  take  work  of
Scorers/Openers/checkers, the respondent will be offered the
job. The net result is that the direction of the Tribunal to
engage the petitioner forthwith, has been negated and it has
been  purely  left  to  the  discretion  of  the  department
concerned.
3. Issue notice, returnable on 31st August, 2026.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

Criminal Appeal No.2898/2024 Page 1 of 2

--- Page 630 ---
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA No. 140962/2026 in Criminal Appeal No.2898/2024
1. Heard  Mr.  Puneet  Jain,  learned  senior  counsel
appearing for the applicant/appellant and Mr. Abhimanyu
Singh, learned counsel appearing for the respondent-
State.
2. Learned  senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
applicant/appellant submits that the appeal has already
been admitted.  The applicant/appellant has undergone
more than 5 years in custody.  The appeal is likely to
take some time to be heard.  Learned senior counsel
submits that the case rests on circumstantial evidence.
3. Learned counsel appearing for the respondent-State
vehemently opposes the prayer of the applicant/appellant
for suspension of sentence and the benefit of bail.
4. Having  considered  the  overall  facts  and
circumstances and the fact that the co-accused/Banti has
already been enlarged on bail, we are inclined to suspend
the sentence of the applicant/appellant and enlarge him
on bail, pending disposal of the present appeal.
5. Therefore, the sentence of the applicant/appellant
is suspended and he is directed to be enlarged on bail,
pending disposal of this appeal.
6. The application is disposed of in the above terms.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                          (MANOJ KUMAR)
     DEPUTY REGISTRAR                          COURT MASTER
Criminal Appeal No.2898/2024 Page 2 of 2

1

--- Page 652 ---

M.A. Diary No. 64100/2025
ITEM NO.3                  COURT NO.8                  SECTION IV-D
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION Diary No. 64100/2025
in
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) No. 120/2025
POONAM                                  Applicant(s)/Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
MAJOR KSHITIJ MAAN                   Non-Applicant(s)/Respondent(s)
(IA  No.  285134/2025  –  CLARIFICATION/DIRECTION  and  IA  No.
285137/2025 - PERMISSION TO APPEAR AND ARGUE IN PERSON)
 
Date : 22-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJEEV SACHDEVA
For Applicant(s)/Petitioner(s) :  
Applicant/Petitioner-in-person
                    
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the applicant, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
The applicant appears in person. She was the petitioner in
T.P.(C) No. 120/2025.  The said transfer petition was dismissed by
this Court on 13.05.2025.  
By way of this miscellaneous application, the applicant states
that  the  respondent-husband,  Kshitij  Maan,  filed  a  counter
affidavit along with supporting documents, one of which was the
complaint allegedly made by the mother-in-law of the applicant, in
vernacular (Hindi) along with an English translation thereof.  
The grievance of the applicant presently is that the said
translation was not in conformity with the original and something
1

--- Page 682 ---
M.A. Diary No. 64100/2025
more has been added therein, which is now being put to use adverse
to the interest of the applicant.  
A copy of the present miscellaneous application was forwarded
to the respondent-husband, Kshitij Maan, by way of email.  Proof of
such service is also on record.  No one appears on his behalf.
In  any  event,  we  are  not  inclined  to  entertain  this
miscellaneous application for further enquiry, as sought by the
applicant.  We, however, make it clear that while dismissing the
transfer petition, this Court did not go into the veracity of the
contents of the said complaint.  No reliance was placed on the said
complaint while dismissing the transfer petition. In consequence,
the occasion did not arise for this Court to even examine whether
the translation was a true reproduction of the Hindi original.
Making this position clear, the miscellaneous application is
disposed of.
(DEEPAK GUGLANI)                             (PREETI SAXENA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                         COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 683 ---

ITEM NO.29               COURT NO.16               SECTION XI
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No(s). 22177/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 09-06-2026
in MUA227 No. 8282/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad]
HEMANT SARAN                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
URMILA DEVI & ORS.                                 Respondent(s)
[ONLY IA 189957/26 IS LISTED UNDER THIS ITEM] 
IA No. 189957/2026 - APPROPRIATE ORDERS/DIRECTIONS
 
Date : 22-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Pukhramban Ramesh Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Nagendra Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Surjeet Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Akansha, AOR
                   Ms. Rajkumari Divyasana, Adv.
                   Mr. Karun Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Naman Raj Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Parth Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Anjali Saxena, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Malak Manish Bhatt, AOR
                   Mr. Prithviraj Dey, Adv.
                   
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The present petition is arising out of the order
dated 09.06.2026, passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Allahabad in Matters Under Article 227 No. 8282 of 2026.
2. The facts of the case reveal that the High Court,
1

--- Page 698 ---
while  hearing  a  petition  under  Article  227  of  the
Constitution of India, has remanded the matter back to the
Appellate Court for hearing on the question of grant of
interim  injunction/injunction,  and  the  High  Court  has
granted an interim injunction in the matter without issuing
notice to the other side.
3. In  the  considered  opinion  of  this  Court,  as
principles of natural justice and fair play have been
violated since the injunction has been granted without
hearing the other side, the order passed by the High Court,
to the extent an injunction has been granted in the matter,
is hereby set aside.
4. It has been brought to the notice of this Court that
the matter is pending before the First Appellate Court and
the application for grant of injunction is also pending.
5. The First Appellate Court shall pass an appropriate
order in accordance with law after granting an opportunity
to both sides on the application for grant of injunction,
and decide the same afresh.
6. With  the  above  observations,  the  Special  Leave
Petition stands disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
  (RAHUL KUMAR)                                    (KOMAL)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Padmesh Mishra, Adv.
                   Ms. Vastvikta Bhardwaj, Adv.
                   Mr. Vijant, Adv.
                   Ms. Neelam Singh, AOR                   
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Ayushi Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Nirajan Marde, Adv.
                   Ms. Shruti Pandey, Adv.
                   Ms. Aswathi M.k., AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1.   We took up this matter today for further hearing in the
Committee Room. Parties are personally present with their
respective counsel.
2.   Prima facie, after talking to the husband, it appears
that things are not good with them at their home. 

--- Page 720 ---
2
3. In pursuance of our last order, the husband and wife
along with their daughter – Garima are now residing separately
on the first floor of the house. 
4. The parents of the husband are on the ground floor.
5. It appears that the in-laws are not maintaining any  
relations with their daughter-in-law.
6. We have conveyed in so many words to the husband whatever
was necessary to be conveyed. 
7.  It is for him now to take the final call. If he does not 
want to save his marriage, it is up to him. But we have made 
ourselves very clear that he will have to pay maintenance of 
reasonable amount to his wife and minor daughter, and we shall
also see to it that the lady and her child reside comfortably 
on the first floor of the house and no one shall disturb them 
in any manner.
8. Post this matter on 30th September, 2026. 
9. We make it clear that on 30th September, 2026, if nothing
works  out  between  the  two,  we  shall  proceed  to  pass
appropriate further order and close this matter.
 (VISHAL ANAND)                                    (POOJA SHARMA)

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) Diary No(s). 65947/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  28-08-2025 in
CRLA No. 802/2024 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New Delhi]
MOHD.SAVEJ                                            Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE (GOVT OF NCT) DELHI                         Respondent(s)
IA No. 325286/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM CUSTODY CERTIFICATE
IA No. 325282/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT And IA No. 325284/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 20-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASHANT KUMAR MISHRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Mohd. Parvez Dabas, Adv.
                   Mr. Uzmi Jameel Husain, Adv.
                   Ms. Qurratulain, AOR
                   Ms. Shagun Mishra, Adv.        
                              
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Anil Kaushik, ASG
                   Mr. Mukesh Kumar Maroria, AOR                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Petitioner has been convicted and sentenced in the
following manner;
S.No Sections Sentence Fine Amount
1 392 of IPC R.I.-04 years Rs.10,000/-
In default whereof, the appellant
was sentenced to undergo S.I. for
06 months
2. 397 of IPC RI-07 years -
3. 411 of IPC RI-01 year Rs.1,000/- 
In default whereof, the appellant
was sentenced to undergo S.I. for
01 month.

--- Page 748 ---
2
2. The conviction and sentence imposed upon
the petitioner has been affirmed by the High Court.
3. In this Special Leave Petition, notice
was issued limited to the quantum of sentence.
However,  during  the  course  of  hearing,  it  is
brought to our notice by Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned
ASG appearing for the respondent that offence under
Section 397 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for
short, ‘IPC’) carries a minimum sentence of 07
years.
4. In view of the above, once the conviction
has been maintained by not entertaining the Special
Leave Petition on merits but, confining it to the
quantum of sentence, this Court, having affirmed
the sentence under Section 397 of IPC, the sentence
cannot be less than the statutory prescription.
5. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition
is dismissed.
6. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall
stand disposed of.
         (NISHA KHULBEY)                    (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
         COURT MASTER(SH)                     COURT MASTER(NSH)

                             O R D E R

counsel appearing for the State, placing reliance on
the medical report , we are not inclined to grant
bail to the petitioner at this stage.

deterioration in the petitioner’s health condition,
liberty is granted to him to file a fresh bail
application before the Trial Court.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  24462/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  25-05-2026
in DBCMA No. 2552/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature for
Rajasthan at Jaipur]
RAJASTHAN STATE ROAD TRANSPORT 
CORPORATION (RSRTC) & ANR. Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
KALPATRU INFRA PROJECT PVT LTD                     Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 205826/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 22-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE  MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s) Mr. B.S. Rajesh Aarajit, Adv.
Mr. Satya Veer Singh, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) Mr. Mrinmay Bhattmewara, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhandari Anchit Amit, AOR
                   Mr. Poonam Chand Bhandari, Adv.
                   Ms. Samprati Bhattmewara, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhinav Bhandari, Adv.
                   Mr. Ayush Raheja, Adv.
                   
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the parties.
The limited grievance of the petitioners is that
in the event of the appeal pending before the High
Court, is allowed, it will be impossible to recover
the amount from the respondent.  

--- Page 814 ---
2
We are inclined to note the fact that the award
is in favour of the respondent, and so also decision
of the learned Single Judge in exercise of power
under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation
Act, 1996.
However, in order to safeguard the interests of
the petitioners, the amount as directed to be paid by
the petitioners to the respondent is subject to
furnishing a bank guarantee for the said amount.  The
needful will have to be done within a period of four
weeks.
On  furnishing  such  bank  guarantee,  the
petitioner(s) shall release the amount as directed by
the High Court.
The Special Leave Petition stand disposed of,
accordingly.
Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.
(ASHA SUNDRIYAL)                                (POONAM VAID)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1.  Delay condoned.
2.  The present Special Leave Petition arises out of the impugned
order dated 11.08.2025 passed by the High Court of Jammu & Kashmir
and Ladakh at Jammu in Crl.M.No.339/2025 in Crl.A.(S) No.6/2025 ,
whereby the High Court granted suspension of the sentence.  The
impugned order dated 11.08.2025 reflects that the High Court listed
the matter on 29.10.2025. Thereafter, the matter has been listed on
several occasions for final hearing; however, the final hearing has
not yet taken place.

--- Page 838 ---
2
3. In view of the fact that the matter is already included in the
final hearing list before the High Court, this Court is not
inclined to interfere with the impugned order. Accordingly, the
Special Leave Petition is dismissed. However, we request the High
Court to decide the appeal at an early date. The question of law is
left open.
4.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
ARBITRATION PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 24 OF 2025
JAMAL UDDIN MAQSOOD AHMAD & 
ANR.
PETITIONER(S)
VERSUS
SYED GHUFFRAN HUSSAIN & ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Counsel for the petitioners, at the outset, pointed out that
Ms. Tanveer Ara, w/o Abdul Wakeel Ansari, is required to join along
with the petitioners.
2. Since there is no conflict of interest between her and the
other petitioners, the prayer is allowed and Ms. Tanveer Ara is
added as co-petitioner. Cause title shall be accordingly amended.
3. Heard learned counsel for the petitioners.
4. The instant arbitration petition is allowed. Justice Asha
Menon, former Judge of the Delhi High Court, is appointed as sole
arbitrator.   All  the  contentions  including  arbitrability  and
maintainability sought to be raised on behalf of the contesting
respondents as well as on behalf of the petitioners are permitted
to be raised before the learned Arbitrator. It is clarified that we

ITEM NO.38                   COURT NO.4                SECTION IV-B
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No.23232/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 08-05-2026 
in CWP No. 14169/2026 passed by the High Court of Punjab & Haryana 
at Chandigarh]
STATE OF PUNJAB & ORS.                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
AKAAL SAHAYE WORKSHOP                                 Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 194726/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 17-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE M.M. SUNDRESH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRASANNA B. VARALE
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Vivek Jain, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Karan Sharma, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioner(s).
Learned counsel for the petitioners submitted that
the grievance of the writ petitioner is only with respect
to one bus. However, an interim order has been passed by
the High Court, ex-parte staying the re-tendering process,
thus touching upon 99 other buses.
Considering the said submission, we only clarify
that the impugned order passed by the High Court shall
only  pertain  to  the  tender  that  the  respondent  is
1

--- Page 880 ---
concerned with. We are conscious of the fact that we are
passing this order ex-parte. 
This  order  shall  continue  to  operate  till
28.07.2026, which is the next date of hearing before the
High Court.
We make it clear that in so far as the other 99
buses are concerned, the process of re-tendering can go on
subject to further orders to be passed in the pending writ
petition which is likely to be listed on 28.07.2026.
We further add that this order will not enable the
petitioner to award the tender, for which appropriate
orders will have to be obtained from the High Court.
The parties are at liberty to raise all their
contentions on the next date of hearing before the High
Court.
The  Special  Leave  Petition  stands  disposed  of,
accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
(KRITIKA TIWARI)                                  (POONAM VAID)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
2

--- Page 881 ---

CIVIL APPEAL NOS.   759-764 OF 2026  
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ORS. ETC.      PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
ANURAG & ORS. ETC.                         RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Application seeking personal hearing in open Court
is rejected.
2. We have perused the review petitions as well as the
grounds in support thereof  and are convinced that the
judgment dated 04.02.2026, of which review has been
sought,  does  not  suffer  from  any  error  apparent
warranting its reconsideration. 
3. The review petitions are, accordingly, dismissed.
4. Pending application(s) also stand disposed of.
....................,J.
       (VIKRAM NATH)
....................,J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
